# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 In-Depth Analysis of Redundancy

### Detection of Redundancy
A constraint is redundant if the linear programming problem remains feasible and has the same optimal value upon its removal. There are three systematic detection approaches:

**Approach 1: Direct Comparison (Graphical Method)**
- Plot all constraints.
- Visually identify constraints that lie strictly outside the feasible polygon.
- These are redundant.

**Approach 2: Using the Dual (Algebraic)**
- The dual of an LPP gives the **shadow prices** (dual variables $y_i$).
- A constraint $i$ is redundant if and only if its dual variable $y_i^{*} = 0$ in **all** optimal solutions.

**Approach 3: Solving an Auxiliary LPP**
- For constraint $\mathbf{a}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_{k}$, solve:
$$\max \, \mathbf{a}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{x} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{x} \in S$$
- If the maximum value $\leq b_k$, the constraint is redundant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Redundancy Matter in Simplex?**
> If a redundant constraint is included in the initial tableau, one of the slack/surplus variables will automatically become zero in every basic feasible solution. This produces **artificial degeneracy**, which can cause the simplex method to perform extra iterations without genuine progress.

---

## 2.2 In-Depth Analysis of Degeneracy

### Algebraic Origin of Degeneracy
Degeneracy occurs when a constraint passes through a vertex of the feasible region that would otherwise be formed by other constraints. At such a vertex, the system $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b}$ produces a basic variable equal to zero, even though it is a basic variable.

### Cycling — The Most Critical Consequence
**Cycling** is a pathological phenomenon where the simplex algorithm oscillates among a finite set of degenerate BFSs without improving the objective function. The sequence of tableaus repeats indefinitely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cycling does NOT occur in practice for most real-world problems**, but it is mathematically possible. KTU examiners may ask a 3-mark question on "How is cycling prevented?"

### Methods to Prevent Cycling
1. **Bland's Rule (Smallest Subscript Rule):**
   - For entering variable: choose the smallest-indexed variable with $\bar{c}_j < 0$ (for minimization, $> 0$).
   - For leaving variable: choose the smallest-indexed variable among the tied minimum ratio.
   - Bland's rule guarantees termination in finite steps.

2. **Perturbation Method (Lexicographic Method):**
   - Replace $\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{b}(\epsilon) = \mathbf{b} + (\epsilon, \epsilon^2, \ldots, \epsilon^m)^{T}$ for a tiny $\epsilon > 0$.
   - This breaks ties in the minimum ratio rule and avoids cycling.

3. **Lexicographic Pivot Rule:**
   - In tied minimum ratio cases, choose the leaving variable by comparing rows lexicographically.

---

## 2.3 In-Depth Analysis of Sensitivity Analysis

### The Four Standard Cases

#### Case 1: Change in Non-Basic Objective Coefficient $c_j$
For a non-basic variable $x_j$ with current reduced cost $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j$, the variable remains non-basic (i.e., the current solution remains optimal) as long as:
$$\bar{c}_j \geq 0 \quad (\text{for minimization})$$
$$\bar{c}_j \leq 0 \quad (\text{for maximization})$$

The allowable **increase** is:
$$\Delta c_j^{max} = \bar{c}_j \quad \text{(for max, when } \bar{c}_j < 0\text{)}$$

#### Case 2: Change in Basic Objective Coefficient $c_r$
For a basic variable $x_{B_r}$, changing its coefficient affects the entire $\mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ row. The new reduced costs are:
$$\bar{c}_j^{new} = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{new,T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j$$

The allowable range is determined by:
$$\Delta c_r^{max} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{rj} > 0} \frac{\bar{c}_j}{\bar{y}_{rj}}, \quad \Delta c_r^{min} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{rj} < 0} \frac{-\bar{c}_j}{-\bar{y}_{rj}}$$

where $\bar{y}_{rj}$ is the element in row $r$ of the optimal simplex tableau.

#### Case 3: Change in RHS Coefficient $b_i$
The new basic solution is:
$$\mathbf{x}_B^{new} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}^{new}$$

The current basis remains optimal (only feasibility changes) as long as all components of $\mathbf{x}_B^{new}$ remain non-negative. The allowable range is:
$$\Delta b_i^{max} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji} > 0} \frac{x_{B_j}}{\bar{y}_{ji}}, \quad \Delta b_i^{min} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji} < 0} \frac{x_{B_j}}{-\bar{y}_{ji}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shadow Price:** The optimal dual variable $y_i^{*} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{e}_i$ gives the change in objective value per unit change in $b_i$, valid over the allowable range.

#### Case 4: Addition of a New Variable
For a new variable $x_{n+1}$ with column $\mathbf{a}_{n+1}$ and coefficient $c_{n+1}$:
$$\bar{c}_{n+1} = c_{n+1} - \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{a}_{n+1}$$
- If $\bar{c}_{n+1} \geq 0$ (max), current solution is still optimal.
- If $\bar{c}_{n+1} < 0$ (max), re-enter the simplex with the new column.

#### Case 5: Addition of a New Constraint
- Substitute the current optimal solution into the new constraint.
- If satisfied, current solution remains optimal.
- If violated, add the new constraint and use the **dual simplex method** to restore feasibility.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Redundant Constraint | $\max_{\mathbf{x} \in S} \mathbf{a}_{k}^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_k$ | Simplifies large-scale models |
| 2 | Degenerate BFS | At least one $x_{B_i} = 0$ in BFS | Identifies cycling risk |
| 3 | Reduced Cost | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j$ | Profit margin for non-basic activity |
| 4 | Shadow Price | $y_i^{*} = (\mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1})_i$ | Value of one extra unit of resource $i$ |
| 5 | RHS Allowable Increase | $\Delta b_i^{max} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji}>0} \dfrac{x_{B_j}}{\bar{y}_{ji}}$ | Capacity expansion limit |
| 6 | RHS Allowable Decrease | $\Delta b_i^{min} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji}<0} \dfrac{x_{B_j}}{-\bar{y}_{ji}}$ | Resource reduction tolerance |
| 7 | $c_j$ (non-basic) Range | $\Delta c_j^{max} = \bar{c}_j$ | Profit margin tolerance |
| 8 | $c_{B_r}$ (basic) Allow. Inc. | $\Delta c_r^{max} = \min\limits_{j:\bar{y}_{rj}>0} \dfrac{\bar{c}_j}{\bar{y}_{rj}}$ | Profit change without re-optimization |
| 9 | $c_{B_r}$ (basic) Allow. Dec. | $\Delta c_r^{min} = \min\limits_{j:\bar{y}_{rj}<0} \dfrac{\bar{c}_j}{\bar{y}_{rj}}$ | Profit change without re-optimization |
| 10 | Cycling Prevention | Bland's Rule (smallest subscript) | Ensures finite termination |
| 11 | New Variable Test | $\bar{c}_{n+1} = c_{n+1} - \mathbf{y}^{*}\mathbf{a}_{n+1}$ | Product portfolio decision |
| 12 | Dual Simplex Trigger | New constraint violated at optimum | Restores feasibility rapidly |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Planning:** Shadow prices reveal the marginal value of extra machine hours.
- **Supply Chain:** Sensitivity on $b_i$ tells the manager how much buffer stock to maintain.
- **Finance:** Sensitivity on $c_j$ measures risk exposure to profit margin fluctuations.
- **Transportation:** Redundancy detection simplifies network flow problems.
- **Energy Systems:** Degeneracy detection avoids computational loops in large-scale LP solvers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Modern solvers like CPLEX and Gurobi internally apply Bland's rule as a default anti-cycling safeguard. Sensitivity reports (ranging information) are generated as a built-in feature of these solvers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Condition for Redundancy

### Setup
Consider a linear program:
$$\max \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{x} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \leq \mathbf{b}, \, \mathbf{x} \geq 0$$

Suppose the $k^{th}$ constraint is $\mathbf{a}_k^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_k$. We want to determine if this constraint is redundant.

### Derivation
Define the feasible set of the **remaining** constraints (excluding the $k^{th}$ one) as:

$$S = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \mid \mathbf{a}_i^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_i, \, i \neq k, \, \mathbf{x} \geq 0 \}$$

The $k^{th}$ constraint is redundant if every point in $S$ automatically satisfies $\mathbf{a}_k^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_k$. This is equivalent to:

$$\max_{\mathbf{x} \in S} \mathbf{a}_k^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_k$$

If the maximum possible value of the LHS over $S$ does not exceed $b_k$, the constraint never "binds" and is redundant.

**Conclusion:** Constraint $k$ is redundant iff the auxiliary LP $\max \mathbf{a}_k^{T}\mathbf{x}$ subject to $\mathbf{x} \in S$ has optimal value $\leq b_k$.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: Condition for Degeneracy

### Setup
Consider the standard form LP with $m$ constraints and $n$ variables. A BFS corresponds to a basis $B$ (an $m \times m$ invertible submatrix of $A$). The basic solution is:

$$\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{x}_N = 0$$

### Derivation
In a BFS, we set $n - m$ non-basic variables to zero. For a **non-degenerate** BFS, we require **all** $m$ basic variables to be strictly positive:
$$x_{B_i} = (\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b})_i > 0 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

**Degeneracy** occurs when this positivity fails — when at least one basic variable equals zero despite being designated as basic:

$$\exists \, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\} : x_{B_i} = (\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b})_i = 0$$

The degree of degeneracy is defined as:
$$\text{deg} = m - |\{i : x_{B_i} > 0\}|$$

### Example (Geometric)
Two constraints $x_1 + x_2 \leq 1$ and $2x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 2$ define the **same** line. The second is redundant but if we force it into the model, the slack variable of the second becomes zero at the optimum — this is **artificial degeneracy**.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation: Allowable Range for $b_i$ (Case 3)

### Setup
Let $\mathbf{B}$ be the optimal basis. The optimal basic solution is $\mathbf{x}_B^{*} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$. Now suppose $b_i$ changes to $b_i + \Delta b_i$, while all other $b_j$ remain fixed. The new RHS vector is $\mathbf{b}^{new} = \mathbf{b} + \Delta b_i \mathbf{e}_i$, where $\mathbf{e}_i$ is the $i^{th}$ unit vector.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The new basic solution becomes:
$$\mathbf{x}_B^{new} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}(\mathbf{b} + \Delta b_i \mathbf{e}_i) = \mathbf{x}_B^{*} + \Delta b_i \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{e}_i$$

**Step 2:** Let $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{e}_i = (\bar{y}_{1i}, \bar{y}_{2i}, \ldots, \bar{y}_{mi})^{T}$ be the $i^{th}$ column of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$. Then:
$$\mathbf{x}_B^{new} = \mathbf{x}_B^{*} + \Delta b_i \mathbf{d}$$

**Step 3:** For the basis to remain **feasible** (basic variables non-negative), we require:
$$x_{B_j}^{*} + \Delta b_i \bar{y}_{ji} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } j = 1, \ldots, m$$

**Step 4:** Split into two cases based on the sign of $\bar{y}_{ji}$:

- If $\bar{y}_{ji} > 0$: 
$$\Delta b_i \geq -\frac{x_{B_j}^{*}}{\bar{y}_{ji}}$$

- If $\bar{y}_{ji} < 0$: 
$$\Delta b_i \leq -\frac{x_{B_j}^{*}}{\bar{y}_{ji}} = \frac{x_{B_j}^{*}}{-\bar{y}_{ji}}$$

- If $\bar{y}_{ji} = 0$: no constraint on $\Delta b_i$ from row $j$.

**Step 5:** The maximum allowable **increase** is determined by the most restrictive row where $\bar{y}_{ji} > 0$:
$$\Delta b_i^{max} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji} > 0} \frac{x_{B_j}^{*}}{\bar{y}_{ji}}$$

**Step 6:** The maximum allowable **decrease** is determined by the most restrictive row where $\bar{y}_{ji} < 0$:
$$\Delta b_i^{min} = \min_{j: \bar{y}_{ji} < 0} \frac{x_{B_j}^{*}}{-\bar{y}_{ji}}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the allowable range for $b_i$ is:
$$b_i + \Delta b_i^{min} \leq b_i^{new} \leq b_i + \Delta b_i^{max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note:** Within this range, the **dual prices (shadow prices)** remain valid. Outside this range, a new basis becomes optimal and shadow prices change.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Derivation: Allowable Range for $c_j$ (Non-Basic, Case 1)

### Setup
For a non-basic variable $x_j$ (currently $x_j = 0$), its current reduced cost is:
$$\bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j = c_j - \mathbf{y}^{*}\mathbf{a}_j$$

### Derivation
If $c_j$ changes to $c_j + \Delta c_j$, the new reduced cost becomes:
$$\bar{c}_j^{new} = (c_j + \Delta c_j) - \mathbf{y}^{*}\mathbf{a}_j = \bar{c}_j + \Delta c_j$$

For the current solution to remain optimal in a **maximization** problem, we need $\bar{c}_j^{new} \leq 0$ (i.e., no incentive to bring $x_j$ into the basis):
$$\bar{c}_j + \Delta c_j \leq 0 \implies \Delta c_j \leq -\bar{c}_j = \vert \bar{c}_j \vert$$

Therefore:
- **Allowable increase:** $\Delta c_j^{max} = -\bar{c}_j = \vert \bar{c}_j \vert$ (when $\bar{c}_j < 0$ for max)
- **Allowable decrease:** $\Delta c_j^{min} = \infty$ (no lower bound, since decreasing $c_j$ only worsens optimality)

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example: Sensitivity Analysis

### Problem
$$\max Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
subject to:
$$x_1 \leq 4$$
$$2x_2 \leq 12$$
$$3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 18$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

### Optimal Simplex Tableau (Final)

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-1/3$ | 2 |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1/2 | $-1/3$ | 3 |
| $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $-1/3$ | 1/3 | 2 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/2 | 2 | $Z^* = 21$ |

### Sub-Question A: Range for $b_1$ (RHS of first constraint)

From the tableau, the $s_1$ row (corresponding to constraint 1) in the $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ part has:
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = (1, 0, 0)^{T}$$

All components are non-negative, so the allowable **decrease** in $b_1$ is bounded by:
$$\Delta b_1^{min} = -\min_{j: \bar{y}_{j1} > 0} \frac{x_{B_j}}{\bar{y}_{j1}} = -\frac{2}{1} = -2$$

Allowable increase: $\Delta b_1^{max} = +\infty$ (no row forces a positive limit).

**Range for $b_1$:** $b_1 \in [4 - 2, \infty) = [2, \infty)$

### Sub-Question B: Range for $c_1$ (Basic variable)

$x_1$ is basic. The row corresponding to $x_1$ in the $Z_j - c_j$ column for non-basic variables ($s_2, s_3$) is:
- Coefficient of $s_2$: $-1/3$
- Coefficient of $s_3$: $1/3$

If $c_1$ changes by $\Delta c_1$, the new $Z_j - c_j$ for non-basic variables becomes:
$$\bar{c}_{s_2}^{new} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \Delta c_1 \cdot (-\tfrac{1}{3})$$
$$\bar{c}_{s_3}^{new} = 2 + \Delta c_1 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}$$

For optimality, both must remain $\geq 0$:
$$\tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{\Delta c_1}{3} \geq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \leq \tfrac{3}{2}$$
$$2 + \tfrac{\Delta c_1}{3} \geq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \geq -6$$

**Range for $c_1$:** $c_1 \in [3 - 6, 3 + 1.5] = [-3, 4.5]$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms with a suitable example:**
**(a)** Redundant constraint in LPP
**(b)** Degenerate basic feasible solution

### Model Answer:

**(a) Redundant Constraint:** A constraint of a linear programming problem is called **redundant** if its removal does not change the feasible region of the problem. In other words, the constraint is automatically satisfied by every feasible solution to the remaining constraints and hence contributes no additional restriction.

**Example:** Consider the constraints $x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$ and $2x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 20$, with $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$. The second constraint is redundant because it is always satisfied whenever the first one is satisfied (the LHS of the second is exactly twice the LHS of the first, with the same RHS scaling).

**(b) Degenerate BFS:** A basic feasible solution of an LPP is called **degenerate** if at least one of the basic variables is equal to zero. In a non-degenerate BFS, all $m$ basic variables are strictly positive and the remaining $(n - m)$ non-basic variables are zero, where $m$ is the number of constraints and $n$ is the number of variables.

**Example:** For a 2-constraint, 3-variable problem, a degenerate BFS would have only one strictly positive basic variable and one zero basic variable, instead of both basic variables being positive.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is sensitivity analysis in linear programming? List the four standard cases of post-optimality analysis.**

### Model Answer:

**Sensitivity Analysis:** Sensitivity analysis (also called post-optimality analysis) is the study of how the optimal solution of a linear programming problem changes in response to changes in the model parameters — namely, the objective function coefficients $c_j$, the right-hand side values $b_i$, and the technological coefficients $a_{ij}$. It determines the ranges of parameter values over which the current optimal basis remains feasible and/or optimal.

**The Four Standard Cases:**
1. **Change in objective function coefficient $c_j$ (non-basic variable):** Determines the allowable change so that the variable remains non-basic.
2. **Change in objective function coefficient $c_r$ (basic variable):** Determines the allowable change in profit/cost of an activity already in the solution.
3. **Change in right-hand side $b_i$:** Determines the allowable change in resource availability while keeping the current basis optimal.
4. **Change in a constraint coefficient $a_{ij}$ or addition of new variable/constraint:** Examines the impact on the current solution.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Consider the following LPP:**
$$\max Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$$
subject to:
$$2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$$
$$x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**(a)** Solve the LPP using the simplex method to obtain the optimal tableau. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Using the optimal tableau, perform sensitivity analysis to find:
- (i) The range of $b_1$ (RHS of constraint 1) for which the shadow price remains valid.
- (ii) The range of $c_1$ (objective coefficient of $x_1$) for which the current basis remains optimal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Simplex Method**

**Iteration 1:** Initial Tableau (with slack variables $s_1, s_2$)

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 10 | 10/2 = 5 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 6 | 6/1 = 6 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | — | -4 | -3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |

Entering: $x_1$ (most negative). Leaving: $s_1$ (min ratio 5).

**Iteration 2:** Pivot on element 2 (row 1, col 1).

Divide row 1 by 2: $s_1$ row becomes (1, 0.5, 0.5, 0, 5).
Row 2 minus $1 \times$ new row 1: $s_2$ row becomes (0, 0.5, -0.5, 1, 1).

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 4 | 1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0 | 5 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 0.5 | -0.5 | 1 | 1 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | — | 0 | -1 | 2 | 0 | 20 |

Entering: $x_2$ (coefficient -1). Leaving: $s_2$ (min ratio $1/0.5 = 2$).

**Iteration 3:** Pivot on element 0.5 (row 2, col 2).

Divide row 2 by 0.5: $s_2$ row becomes (0, 1, -1, 2, 2).
Row 1 minus $0.5 \times$ new row 2: $x_1$ row becomes (1, 0, 1, -1, 4).

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 4 | 1 | 0 | 1 | -1 | 4 |
| $x_2$ | 3 | 0 | 1 | -1 | 2 | 2 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 22 |

All $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$. **Optimal reached.**

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 4, x_2 = 2, Z^* = 22$.

**Part (b): Sensitivity Analysis**

**(i) Range of $b_1$:**

The optimal $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ matrix (columns of slack variables) is:
$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

The first column of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ is $\mathbf{d}_1 = (1, -1)^{T}$.
- Row 1 ($x_1$): $\bar{y}_{11} = 1 > 0$. Ratio: $4/1 = 4$.
- Row 2 ($x_2$): $\bar{y}_{21} = -1 < 0$. Ratio: $2/1 = 2$.

**Allowable increase:** $\Delta b_1^{max} = \min(4) = 4$.
**Allowable decrease:** $\Delta b_1^{min} = \min(2) = 2$.

**Range:** $b_1 \in [10 - 2, 10 + 4] = [8, 14]$.

**(ii) Range of $c_1$:**

$x_1$ is a basic variable. The pivot row for $x_1$ in the $Z_j - c_j$ row entries for non-basic variables ($s_1, s_2$):
- Coefficient of $s_1$: $1$
- Coefficient of $s_2$: $-1$

If $c_1$ changes by $\Delta c_1$, new $Z_j - c_j$ values:
- For $s_1$: $1 + \Delta c_1 \cdot 1 = 1 + \Delta c_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \geq -1$.
- For $s_2$: $2 + \Delta c_1 \cdot (-1) = 2 - \Delta c_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \leq 2$.

**Range:** $c_1 \in [4 - 1, 4 + 2] = [3, 6]$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A manufacturing firm produces two products P and Q. The optimal simplex tableau of the corresponding LPP is given below. Use it to answer the following:**

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | -1 | 2 |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | -1 | 3 |
| $x_2$ | 4 | 0 | 1 | 0 | -1 | 1 | 4 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 26 |

**(a)** Identify whether the current basic feasible solution is **degenerate** or **non-degenerate**. Justify. **(3 marks)**
**(b)** Find the **allowable range** for $b_2$ (RHS of constraint 2) and $b_3$ (RHS of constraint 3). **(6 marks)**
**(c)** Suppose a new variable $x_4$ with $c_4 = 6$ and column $\mathbf{a}_4 = (2, 1, 1)^{T}$ is introduced. Will the current solution remain optimal? **(5 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Degeneracy Check**

The BFS has basic variables: $x_1 = 2, s_1 = 3, x_2 = 4$. All basic variables are strictly positive ($2, 3, 4 > 0$). There are $m = 3$ constraints and 3 basic variables, all positive.

**Conclusion:** The current BFS is **non-degenerate** because all three basic variables are strictly greater than zero.

**Part (b): Allowable Ranges**

The optimal $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ (columns of $s_1, s_2, s_3$ in the rows of basic variables) is:
$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**For $b_2$ (column 2 of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$):** $\mathbf{d}_2 = (1, 2, -1)^{T}$.

- Row 1 ($x_1$): $\bar{y}_{12} = 1 > 0$. Ratio: $2/1 = 2$.
- Row 2 ($s_1$): $\bar{y}_{22} = 2 > 0$. Ratio: $3/2 = 1.5$.
- Row 3 ($x_2$): $\bar{y}_{32} = -1 < 0$. Ratio: $4/1 = 4$.

**Allowable increase:** $\Delta b_2^{max} = \min(2, 1.5) = 1.5$.
**Allowable decrease:** $\Delta b_2^{min} = \min(4) = 4$.

**Range for $b_2$:** $b_2 \in [b_2 - 4, b_2 + 1.5]$.

**For $b_3$ (column 3 of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$):** $\mathbf{d}_3 = (-1, -1, 1)^{T}$.

- Row 1 ($x_1$): $\bar{y}_{13} = -1 < 0$. Ratio: $2/1 = 2$.
- Row 2 ($s_1$): $\bar{y}_{23} = -1 < 0$. Ratio: $3/1 = 3$.
- Row 3 ($x_2$): $\bar{y}_{33} = 1 > 0$. Ratio: $4/1 = 4$.

**Allowable increase:** $\Delta b_3^{max} = \min(4) = 4$.
**Allowable decrease:** $\Delta b_3^{min} = \min(2, 3) = 2$.

**Range for $b_3$:** $b_3 \in [b_3 - 2, b_3 + 4]$.

**Part (c): New Variable $x_4$ Test**

Current optimal dual variables (from $Z_j - c_j$ row, columns of original slack variables):
$$\mathbf{y}^{*} = (0, 1, 1)$$

Reduced cost of new variable:
$$\bar{c}_4 = c_4 - \mathbf{y}^{*}\mathbf{a}_4 = 6 - (0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1) = 6 - 2 = 4$$

Since $\bar{c}_4 = 4 > 0$ (for maximization), the new variable would not improve the objective function. **Current solution remains optimal.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often mix up $\bar{c}_j = c_j - z_j$ and $\bar{c}_j = z_j - c_j$. For maximization, optimality requires $z_j - c_j \geq 0$ (or equivalently $c_j - z_j \leq 0$). **Loses 2 marks per question.**
>
> 2. **Confusing "Optimality Range" vs "Feasibility Range":** For $b_i$ changes, the range is a **feasibility** range (basic solution remains non-negative). For $c_j$ changes, the range is an **optimality** range (reduced costs remain valid). Mixing these up is a common 3-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Update $c_B$:** When $c_j$ for a **basic** variable changes, the entire $Z_j - c_j$ row must be recomputed using the new $c_B$. Students often forget and only check one column. **Loses 4 marks.**
>
> 4. **Not Showing Pivot Row Analysis:** The allowable range for basic $c_r$ requires examining ALL non-basic variables' $Z_j - c_j$ values with their pivot row coefficients. Skipping this loses 3-5 marks.
>
> 5. **Cycling vs Degeneracy Confusion:** Cycling is a **consequence** of degeneracy, not the same as degeneracy. A degenerate BFS does not always cycle. **Loses 2 marks in definition questions.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Redundancy:** A constraint is redundant if removing it does not change the feasible region. Detected via $\max_{\mathbf{x} \in S} \mathbf{a}_k^{T}\mathbf{x} \leq b_k$ or by zero shadow price.
- [ ] **Degeneracy:** A BFS is degenerate if any basic variable is zero. Algebraically, $\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ has a zero component.
- [ ] **Cycling Risk:** Degeneracy can lead to cycling. Prevented by **Bland's Rule** (smallest subscript for both entering and leaving variables).
- [ ] **Perturbation Method:** Replace $\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{b}(\epsilon) = \mathbf{b} + (\epsilon, \epsilon^2, \ldots, \epsilon^m)^{T}$ to break ties.
- [ ] **Sensitivity on $c_j$ (Non-Basic):** Range is $\Delta c_j \leq \vert \bar{c}_j \vert$ (max problem). No lower bound.
- [ ] **Sensitivity on $c_r$ (Basic):** Range requires checking ALL non-basic variables' reduced costs.
- [ ] **Sensitivity on $b_i$:** Use $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ columns. Allowable increase is min of $x_{B_j}/\bar{y}_{ji}$ for $\bar{y}_{ji} > 0$.
- [ ] **Shadow Price Validity:** Valid ONLY within the allowable $b_i$ range. Outside this range, a new basis is needed.
- [ ] **New Variable Test:** Compute $\bar{c}_{n+1} = c_{n+1} - \mathbf{y}^{*}\mathbf{a}_{n+1}$. If non-positive (max), current solution is optimal.
- [ ] **New Constraint Test:** Substitute current solution; if violated, apply **dual simplex method** to restore feasibility.
- [ ] **Reduced Cost Interpretation:** $\bar{c}_j$ is the amount by which $c_j$ must improve before $x_j$ enters the optimal basis.
- [ ] **Shadow Price Interpretation:** $y_i^*$ is the marginal value of one additional unit of resource $i$.
- [ ] **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "current basis remains optimal" or "current basis remains feasible") explicitly in sensitivity answers.
- [ ] **Formula Memory Aid:** "Numerator is current value, denominator is the column entry — sign of denominator decides increase or decrease."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation and Assignment Problems: Transportation problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT504 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transportation and Assignment Problems: Transportation problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Transportation Problem (TP)** is a special class of Linear Programming Problems (LPP) in which the objective is to determine an optimal shipping schedule that minimizes the total cost of transporting a homogeneous commodity from multiple **sources (origins / factories)** to multiple **destinations (warehouses / markets)**, subject to the supply constraints at each origin and the demand requirements at each destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard Textbook Terminology):**
> The Transportation Problem deals with the distribution of a single product from a group of sources to a group of destinations. Sources are points of supply (e.g., factories, warehouses) and destinations are points of demand (e.g., retail outlets, depots). The objective is to determine the quantity to be shipped from each source to each destination such that the total transportation cost is minimized.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Sources / Origins | $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$ | Points of supply (factories) |
| Destinations | $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ | Points of demand (warehouses) |
| Supply at source $i$ | $s_i$ or $a_i$ | Available units at origin $i$ |
| Demand at destination $j$ | $d_j$ or $b_j$ | Required units at destination $j$ |
| Unit transportation cost | $c_{ij}$ | Cost of shipping one unit from $i$ to $j$ |
| Quantity shipped | $x_{ij}$ | Decision variable (units to be shipped) |
| **Balanced TP** | $\sum s_i = \sum d_j$ | Total supply equals total demand |
| **Unbalanced TP** | $\sum s_i \neq \sum d_j$ | Dummy source/destination introduced |

### 1.2 Mathematical Formulation

**Decision Variables:** $x_{ij} =$ quantity of commodity shipped from source $i$ to destination $j$.

**Objective Function:** Minimize the total transportation cost

$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$

**Subject to the supply constraints:**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = s_i, \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

**Subject to the demand constraints:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = d_j, \quad \forall \, j = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Non-negativity restriction:**

$$x_{ij} \geq 0, \quad \forall \, i, j$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transportation problem has $(m + n)$ constraints, but only $(m + n - 1)$ of them are **linearly independent** (one equation is redundant). Therefore, any **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** will contain exactly $(m + n - 1)$ positive basic variables, and the rest will be zero (non-basic).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Kerala Spices Distributor 🛳️**
> Imagine you run a spice-trading company in Kerala with **3 godowns** in Kochi, Thrissur, and Kozhikode (sources) that stock cardamom. You have **4 wholesale customers** in Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, and Bangalore (destinations) who need specific quantities. Each route (Kochi → Delhi, Thrissur → Mumbai, etc.) has a **different shipping cost** depending on distance, transport mode, and taxes.
> - Your **decision:** How many tonnes of cardamom should be sent on each route?
> - Your **goal:** Satisfy every customer's demand, never exceed any godown's stock, and pay the **minimum total freight**.
> - The Transportation Problem is the mathematical engine that solves exactly this puzzle in seconds!

This is precisely why every Operations Research course (and KTU's PBCBT504) treats it as a **Module 2 cornerstone** — it appears in logistics, supply chain management, production planning, and even workforce deployment scenarios.

### 1.4 The Transportation Tableau

The most convenient representation is the **Transportation Tableau**, which is a compact matrix form of the LPP.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & \cdots & D_n & \text{Supply } (s_i) \\
\hline
S_1 & c_{11} & c_{12} & \cdots & c_{1n} & s_1 \\
S_2 & c_{21} & c_{22} & \cdots & c_{2n} & s_2 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
S_m & c_{m1} & c_{m2} & \cdots & c_{mn} & s_m \\
\hline
\text{Demand } (d_j) & d_1 & d_2 & \cdots & d_n & \text{Total} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balanced vs. Unbalanced — KTU Standard Fix:**
> If $\sum s_i > \sum d_j$, add a **dummy destination** with demand equal to the surplus supply (with zero shipping cost on all its rows).
> If $\sum s_i < \sum d_j$, add a **dummy source** with supply equal to the shortage (with zero shipping cost on all its columns).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two-Phase Solution Strategy

The Transportation Problem is solved in two clear phases (this is the **standard KTU exam pattern**):

**Phase 1 — Obtain an Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS):** Choose $(m + n - 1)$ basic cells satisfying all supply/demand constraints.
**Phase 2 — Test for Optimality & Iterate:** Use MODI or Stepping Stone method to verify optimality; if not optimal, iterate to improve the cost.

> [!NOTE]
> The IBFS is *not* guaranteed to be optimal — it is just a starting point. Three popular methods to find an IBFS are described below.

### 2.2 Phase 1: Methods to Find the Initial Basic Feasible Solution

#### (a) North-West Corner Method (NWCM) — Most Mechanical
1. Start at the **top-left cell** $(S_1, D_1)$.
2. Allocate the maximum possible: $\min(s_1, d_1)$.
3. Move **right** if the column demand is met; move **down** if the row supply is exhausted.
4. Repeat until all supply and demand are satisfied.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use it?** Quickest, no cost consideration. **Drawback?** Ignores cost — typically the most expensive IBFS of all three methods.

#### (b) Least Cost Method (LCM) — Cost-Sensitive
1. Identify the cell with the **lowest $c_{ij}$** in the entire matrix.
2. Allocate $\min(s_i, d_j)$ to it.
3. Strike out the exhausted row or column; repeat with the lowest remaining cost.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** LCM usually gives a better (lower) initial cost than NWCM but is still suboptimal in many cases.

#### (c) Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) — Most Accurate IBFS 🏆
VAM almost always yields the **best IBFS** and is the **most frequently asked method in KTU exams**.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. For **each row** and **each column**, compute the **penalty** = (second smallest cost) − (smallest cost).
2. Identify the **row or column with the maximum penalty**.
3. In that row/column, choose the cell with the **lowest cost**.
4. Allocate $\min(s_i, d_j)$ to that cell.
5. Strike out the satisfied row/column. Adjust the residual supply/demand.
6. Recompute penalties (excluding struck-out lines) and repeat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why VAM works:** The penalty represents the *opportunity cost* of not using the cheapest cell. A high penalty warns that a poor choice will dramatically increase total cost. By allocating to the lowest cost cell in the highest-penalty line, VAM hedges against the worst-case mistake.

### 2.3 Phase 2: Optimality Test Methods

#### (a) MODI Method (Modified Distribution Method / $u_i v_j$ Method) — KTU Favourite ✅

**Why "Modified"?** It is a streamlined version of the dual simplex approach tailored for the transportation tableau.

**Algorithm:**

1. For each basic cell $(i,j)$, set up the linear equation: $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$.
2. Set $u_1 = 0$ (arbitrary reference) and solve for all $u_i$ and $v_j$ — there will be exactly $(m + n - 1)$ equations in $(m + n)$ unknowns, so a unique solution is obtained.
3. For every **non-basic cell**, compute the **opportunity (reduced) cost:**

$$
\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j
$$

4. **Optimality Rule:**
   - If all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$, the current BFS is **optimal** ✅
   - If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, the solution is **not optimal**. Pick the most negative $\Delta_{ij}$ as the **entering variable** and improve via a closed loop.

5. **Closed Loop Construction:** Form a closed path (rectangle / polygon) starting and ending at the entering cell, using only allocated cells as corners, with horizontal/vertical moves, and exactly one corner in each row and column.

6. Mark `+` at the entering cell, alternate `−` and `+` along the loop, and identify the **leaving variable** = the basic cell with the smallest allocation among the `−` corners.

7. Add $\theta$ to `+` cells and subtract $\theta$ from `−` cells; recompute and re-test.

#### (b) Stepping Stone Method
- A path-tracing method where you directly evaluate the impact of bringing one unit into an empty cell by tracing a closed path through occupied cells.
- Slower than MODI but conceptually equivalent. **KTU Tip:** MODI is preferred because it uses a single set of $u_i, v_j$ for all empty cells, whereas Stepping Stone requires tracing a separate path for each empty cell.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of basic variables (BFS) | $m + n - 1$ | Always check **degeneracy** against this |
| **Degeneracy** | BFS has fewer than $m + n - 1$ occupied cells | Infinitely many optimal solutions possible |
| Degeneracy fix | Place a tiny $\varepsilon$ in an unoccupied cell to make $m+n-1$ allocations | **Most common mistake in KTU exams** is ignoring degeneracy |
| Balanced condition | $\sum s_i = \sum d_j$ | If violated, introduce dummy row/column |
| MODI equation | $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for basic cells | Set $u_1 = 0$ to anchor the system |
| Reduced cost | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for non-basic cells | $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0 \;\forall$ non-basic $\Rightarrow$ optimal |
| Entering cell | Most negative $\Delta_{ij}$ | The "biggest opportunity" to reduce cost |
| Leaving cell | Smallest allocation on `−` corners of closed loop | Ensures non-negativity after pivoting |
| **Prohibited route** | $c_{ij} = \infty$ or $M$ (very large) | Cells where shipment is not allowed |
| **Maximization TP** | Convert: $c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$, where $M = \max(c_{ij})$ | Then solve as minimization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Bar in Tables — Examiner's LaTeX Pitfall:** When writing absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or determinant $\vert A \vert$ inside a markdown table, **never** use the pipe character `|`. Always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`. Otherwise, the markdown parser will treat it as a column separator and break the table layout.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

The Transportation Problem is foundational to:

- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Optimal goods flow from factories → warehouses → retail stores.
- **Production Planning:** Allocates finished goods inventory from multiple plants to distribution centres.
- **Defense & Military:** Troop deployment and equipment movement planning.
- **Agriculture:** Distributing perishables (e.g., vegetables, milk) from collection centres to processing plants.
- **Healthcare:** Distributing vaccines/blood units from regional centres to hospitals.
- **Energy Sector:** Coal dispatch from mines to power plants; crude oil from ports to refineries.
- **Workforce Assignment:** Closely related to the Assignment Problem (covered next in the module).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Numerical Walkthrough — VAM + MODI

We will solve a **3-source × 4-destination balanced Transportation Problem** end-to-end. This is a representative KTU-style question.

**Problem Statement:**

A logistics company has three warehouses $S_1, S_2, S_3$ with supplies **100, 150, 50** units respectively, and four retail outlets $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ with demands **75, 125, 75, 25** units. The per-unit shipping cost (in ₹) is given by the matrix below. Find the **optimal transportation plan** that minimizes total cost.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
S_1 & 5 & 3 & 2 & 6 & 100 \\
S_2 & 4 & 7 & 5 & 8 & 150 \\
S_3 & 6 & 2 & 4 & 9 & 50 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 75 & 125 & 75 & 25 & 300 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Verification of Balance:** $\sum s_i = 100 + 150 + 50 = 300 = \sum d_j = 75 + 125 + 75 + 25$ ✓ Balanced.

---

#### PHASE 1: Initial BFS using Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)

##### Iteration 1

**Step 1:** Compute row and column penalties (difference between the two smallest costs).

- **Row $S_1$:** Sorted costs $(2, 3, 5, 6)$ → Penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$
- **Row $S_2$:** Sorted costs $(4, 5, 7, 8)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Row $S_3$:** Sorted costs $(2, 4, 6, 9)$ → Penalty $= 4 - 2 = 2$
- **Column $D_1$:** Sorted costs $(4, 5, 6)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column $D_2$:** Sorted costs $(2, 3, 7)$ → Penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$
- **Column $D_3$:** Sorted costs $(2, 4, 5)$ → Penalty $= 4 - 2 = 2$
- **Column $D_4$:** Sorted costs $(6, 8, 9)$ → Penalty $= 8 - 6 = 2$

**Step 2:** Maximum penalty = 2 (tied among $S_3, D_3, D_4$). Apply tie-breaking — choose the **first occurring** row/column, i.e., $S_3$.

**Step 3:** In row $S_3$, the lowest cost is $2$ at cell $(S_3, D_2)$.

**Step 4:** Allocate $\min(50, 125) = 50$ to $(S_3, D_2)$. Row $S_3$ supply is now exhausted — **delete row $S_3$**.

**Reduced table after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
S_1 & 5 & 3 & 2 & 6 & 100 \\
S_2 & 4 & 7 & 5 & 8 & 150 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 75 & 75 & 75 & 25 & 300 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

##### Iteration 2

**Step 1:** Recompute penalties on the reduced table.

- **Row $S_1$:** $(2, 3, 5, 6)$ → Penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$
- **Row $S_2$:** $(4, 5, 7, 8)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column $D_1$:** $(4, 5)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column $D_2$:** $(3, 7)$ → Penalty $= 7 - 3 = 4$ ✅ (max)
- **Column $D_3$:** $(2, 5)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 2 = 3$
- **Column $D_4$:** $(6, 8)$ → Penalty $= 8 - 6 = 2$

**Step 2:** Maximum penalty = 4 at column $D_2$.

**Step 3:** In column $D_2$, the lowest cost is $3$ at cell $(S_1, D_2)$.

**Step 4:** Allocate $\min(100, 75) = 75$ to $(S_1, D_2)$. Column $D_2$ demand is now fully met — **delete column $D_2$**.

**Reduced table after Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|ccc|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_3 & D_4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
S_1 & 5 & 2 & 6 & 25 \\
S_2 & 4 & 5 & 8 & 150 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 75 & 75 & 25 & 175 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

##### Iteration 3

**Step 1:** Recompute penalties.

- **Row $S_1$:** $(2, 5, 6)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 2 = 3$ ✅ (max)
- **Row $S_2$:** $(4, 5, 8)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column $D_1$:** $(4, 5)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 4 = 1$
- **Column $D_3$:** $(2, 5)$ → Penalty $= 5 - 2 = 3$ ✅ (max)
- **Column $D_4$:** $(6, 8)$ → Penalty $= 8 - 6 = 2$

**Step 2:** Tie in maximum penalty (3) at $S_1$ and $D_3$. Apply tie-breaking rule → choose **$S_1$**.

**Step 3:** In row $S_1$, the lowest cost is $2$ at cell $(S_1, D_3)$.

**Step 4:** Allocate $\min(25, 75) = 25$ to $(S_1, D_3)$. Row $S_1$ supply is now exhausted — **delete row $S_1$**.

**Reduced table after Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|ccc|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_3 & D_4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
S_2 & 4 & 5 & 8 & 150 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 75 & 50 & 25 & 150 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

##### Iteration 4 (Trivial)

Only one source $S_2$ remains. Allocate directly:

- $(S_2, D_1) = \min(150, 75) = 75$
- $(S_2, D_3) = \min(75, 50) = 50$
- $(S_2, D_4) = \min(25, 25) = 25$

##### Summary of VAM Allocations (Initial BFS)

| Cell | Allocation $x_{ij}$ | Cost $c_{ij}$ | Cost Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(S_1, D_2)$ | 75 | 3 | 225 |
| $(S_1, D_3)$ | 25 | 2 | 50 |
| $(S_2, D_1)$ | 75 | 4 | 300 |
| $(S_2, D_3)$ | 50 | 5 | 250 |
| $(S_2, D_4)$ | 25 | 8 | 200 |
| $(S_3, D_2)$ | 50 | 2 | 100 |

**Total Number of Basic Cells = 6 = $m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1$ ✓ (Non-degenerate)**

**Initial Total Cost:**

$$Z_{\text{initial}} = 225 + 50 + 300 + 250 + 200 + 100 = 1125$$

---

#### PHASE 2: Optimality Test using MODI Method

##### Step 1: Set up $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for all basic cells

With the convention $u_1 = 0$, we solve the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 + v_2 &= c_{12} = 3 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad 0 + v_2 &= 3 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad v_2 &= 3 \\
u_1 + v_3 &= c_{13} = 2 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad 0 + v_3 &= 2 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad v_3 &= 2 \\
u_2 + v_1 &= c_{21} = 4 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad u_2 + v_1 &= 4 \\
u_2 + v_3 &= c_{23} = 5 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad u_2 + 2 &= 5 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad u_2 &= 3 \\
u_2 + v_4 &= c_{24} = 8 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad 3 + v_4 &= 8 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad v_4 &= 5 \\
u_3 + v_2 &= c_{32} = 2 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad u_3 + 3 &= 2 \quad &\Rightarrow\quad u_3 &= -1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substituting into the unused equation $u_2 + v_1 = 4$:

$$3 + v_1 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow\quad v_1 = 1$$

**Final $u_i, v_j$ values:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|ccc|c|}
\hline
u_1 = 0 & u_2 = 3 & u_3 = -1 & \text{} & \text{} \\
v_1 = 1 & v_2 = 3 & v_3 = 2 & v_4 = 5 & \text{} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

##### Step 2: Compute Opportunity Cost $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for all non-basic cells

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{11} &= c_{11} - u_1 - v_1 = 5 - 0 - 1 = +4 \\
\Delta_{14} &= c_{14} - u_1 - v_4 = 6 - 0 - 5 = +1 \\
\Delta_{22} &= c_{22} - u_2 - v_2 = 7 - 3 - 3 = +1 \\
\Delta_{31} &= c_{31} - u_3 - v_1 = 6 - (-1) - 1 = +6 \\
\Delta_{33} &= c_{33} - u_3 - v_3 = 4 - (-1) - 2 = +3 \\
\Delta_{34} &= c_{34} - u_3 - v_4 = 9 - (-1) - 5 = +5 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Step 3: Optimality Verdict

Since **all** $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$, the current BFS satisfies the optimality condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion: The Initial VAM solution is itself the Optimal Solution!**
> No further iterations are required.

##### Step 4: Optimal Total Cost

$$
\boxed{Z_{\min} = 1125}
$$

##### Final Optimal Transportation Schedule

| From $\downarrow$ / To $\rightarrow$ | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | Supply Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | — | **75** | **25** | — | 100 ✓ |
| $S_2$ | **75** | — | **50** | **25** | 150 ✓ |
| $S_3$ | — | **50** | — | — | 50 ✓ |
| Demand Fulfilled | 75 ✓ | 125 ✓ | 75 ✓ | 25 ✓ | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Insight — Multiple Optimal Solutions:**
> If any non-basic cell had $\Delta_{ij} = 0$, the TP would have **alternate optimal solutions** (infinitely many). The student can detect this by examining the non-basic cells. In our case, all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 1$, so the optimum is **unique**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Full TP Solution Algorithm

The diagram below outlines the complete end-to-end algorithmic pathway for solving any Transportation Problem, as expected in KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read the TP Data m x n Supply s_i and Demand d_j and Cost c_ij"] --> B["Check Balance: Sum s_i equals Sum d_j ?"]
    B -- NO --> C1["UNBALANCED: Add Dummy Row or Column with Zero Cost to Balance the Matrix"]
    C1 --> D["Balance Achieved: Proceed to Phase 1"]
    B -- YES --> D
    D --> E["PHASE 1: Choose IBFS Method NWCM or LCM or VAM"]
    E --> F["Compute Allocations in the Tableau"]
    F --> G["Count Allocated Basic Cells Check equals m + n minus 1 ?"]
    G -- NO --> H["DEGENERACY DETECTED: Insert epsilon in any Zero Cost Unoccupied Cell"]
    H --> I["PHASE 2: Apply Optimality Test MODI Method Preferred"]
    G -- YES --> I
    I --> J["Set u_1 = 0 and Solve u_i + v_j = c_ij for Basic Cells"]
    J --> K["Compute Opportunity Cost Delta_ij = c_ij minus u_i minus v_j for Non Basic Cells"]
    K --> L{"All Delta_ij greater than or equal to Zero ?"}
    L -- YES --> M["OPTIMAL SOLUTION FOUND: Report Allocations and Total Cost Z_min"]
    L -- NO --> N["Pick Entering Cell: Most Negative Delta_ij"]
    N --> O["Construct Closed Loop using Basic Cells as Corners"]
    O --> P["Identify Leaving Cell: Smallest Allocation on Negative Corners"]
    P --> Q["Update Allocations: Add theta to Plus Corners Subtract theta from Minus Corners"]
    Q --> R["New BFS Obtained: Return to MODI Step and Re Test Optimality"]
    R --> K
    M --> S["END: Print Final Transportation Schedule"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Concepts to Methods Mapping

This block diagram shows how the conceptual layers of the Transportation Problem map to the algorithmic methods and the outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["LAYER 1: PROBLEM INPUT Supply and Demand Vectors and Cost Matrix"] --> B1["LAYER 2: BALANCING Dummy Row or Column if Needed"]
    B1 --> C1["LAYER 3: INITIAL BFS GENERATION Three Methods Available NWCM LCM VAM"]
    C1 --> D1["LAYER 4: DEGENERACY CHECK Number of Allocations equals m plus n minus 1"]
    D1 --> E1["LAYER 5: OPTIMALITY TESTING MODI or Stepping Stone"]
    E1 --> F1{"All Reduced Costs Non Negative ?"}
    F1 -- YES --> G1["LAYER 6 OUTPUT: OPTIMAL COST Z_min and Final Allocations x_ij"]
    F1 -- NO --> H1["LAYER 5B: ITERATION Closed Loop Pivoting Update u_i and v_j"]
    H1 --> E1
    G1 --> I1["LAYER 7: VALIDATION Check All Supply and Demand Satisfied"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Model — Sources, Destinations, and Routes

The image below abstracts the physical idea of a transportation network as a bipartite graph that the solver manipulates mathematically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["S1 Supply 100"] -->|Route 11 cost 5| D1["D1 Demand 75"]
    S1 -->|Route 12 cost 3| D2["D2 Demand 125"]
    S1 -->|Route 13 cost 2| D3["D3 Demand 75"]
    S1 -->|Route 14 cost 6| D4["D4 Demand 25"]
    S2["S2 Supply 150"] -->|Route 21 cost 4| D1
    S2 -->|Route 22 cost 7| D2
    S2 -->|Route 23 cost 5| D3
    S2 -->|Route 24 cost 8| D4
    S3["S3 Supply 50"] -->|Route 31 cost 6| D1
    S3 -->|Route 32 cost 2| D2
    S3 -->|Route 33 cost 4| D3
    S3 -->|Route 34 cost 9| D4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The solver's goal is to decide which arcs (routes) to **activate** and with **how much flow** to satisfy the supply/demand at minimum cost. The tableau is just a compact tabular form of the same bipartite graph.
