# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define ABC analysis. State the standard percentage cutoffs used in KTU 2024 Scheme problems for classifying items into A, B, and C categories.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** ABC analysis is an inventory classification technique based on the Pareto Principle. It groups items by their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV = Annual Demand × Unit Cost)**. A small fraction of items (A) carries most of the inventory value, requiring tight control.
- **Cutoffs (1 mark):**
  - **A-Class:** Cumulative value ≤ **70%**
  - **B-Class:** 70% < Cumulative value ≤ **90%**
  - **C-Class:** Cumulative value > **90%**

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the control policies recommended for Class A and Class C items in ABC analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Class A (3 marks):** Tight control — frequent review, low safety stock, EOQ-based ordering, accurate forecasting, detailed record keeping, periodic physical verification, and **small order sizes with frequent orders**.
- **Class C (3 marks):** Loosened control — bulk ordering, large order sizes, minimal record keeping, no formal forecasting, two-bin system, and **rare or no physical verification**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Model Paper 2024]**

> A manufacturing company stores **8 items** with the following details:
>
> | Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
> |------|----------------------|----------------|
> | P1   | 400                  | 20             |
> | P2   | 200                  | 60             |
> | P3   | 1000                 | 8              |
> | P4   | 50                   | 150            |
> | P5   | 300                  | 30             |
> | P6   | 150                  | 45             |
> | P7   | 600                  | 12             |
> | P8   | 80                   | 100            |
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the Annual Consumption Value for each item, sort the items in descending order, and prepare the ABC classification table using the **standard 70/90/100 cutoffs**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Recommend appropriate control policies for each class of items and explain how the firm's working capital tied up in inventory can be reduced through this classification.

#### Solution to Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute ACV = Annual Usage × Unit Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACV}_{P1} &= 400 \times 20 = 8{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P2} &= 200 \times 60 = 12{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P3} &= 1000 \times 8 = 8{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P4} &= 50 \times 150 = 7{,}500 \\
\text{ACV}_{P5} &= 300 \times 30 = 9{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{P6} &= 150 \times 45 = 6{,}750 \\
\text{ACV}_{P7} &= 600 \times 12 = 7{,}200 \\
\text{ACV}_{P8} &= 80 \times 100 = 8{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total**

$$
T = 8000 + 12000 + 8000 + 7500 + 9000 + 6750 + 7200 + 8000 = 66{,}450 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3: Sort in Descending Order of ACV**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) |
|------|------|----------|
| 1    | P2   | 12,000   |
| 2    | P5   | 9,000    |
| 3    | P1   | 8,000    |
| 3    | P3   | 8,000    |
| 3    | P8   | 8,000    |
| 6    | P4   | 7,500    |
| 7    | P7   | 7,200    |
| 8    | P6   | 6,750    |

**Step 4: Percentage and Cumulative Percentage**

$$
p_{P2} = \frac{12000}{66450} \times 100 = 18.06\%
$$

$$
P_{P2} = 18.06\%
$$

$$
p_{P5} = \frac{9000}{66450} \times 100 = 13.54\%
$$

$$
P_{P5} = 18.06 + 13.54 = 31.60\%
$$

$$
p_{P1} = p_{P3} = p_{P8} = \frac{8000}{66450} \times 100 = 12.04\%
$$

$$
P_{P1} = 31.60 + 12.04 = 43.64\%
$$

$$
P_{P3} = 43.64 + 12.04 = 55.68\%
$$

$$
P_{P8} = 55.68 + 12.04 = 67.72\%
$$

$$
p_{P4} = \frac{7500}{66450} \times 100 = 11.29\%
$$

$$
P_{P4} = 67.72 + 11.29 = 79.01\%
$$

$$
p_{P7} = \frac{7200}{66450} \times 100 = 10.83\%
$$

$$
P_{P7} = 79.01 + 10.83 = 89.84\%
$$

$$
p_{P6} = \frac{6750}{66450} \times 100 = 10.16\%
$$

$$
P_{P6} = 89.84 + 10.16 = 100.00\%
$$

**Step 5: Classification Table**

| Item | ACV (₹) | $p_i$ (%) | $P_k$ (%) | Class |
|------|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| P2   | 12,000   | 18.06     | 18.06     | **A**  |
| P5   | 9,000    | 13.54     | 31.60     | **A**  |
| P1   | 8,000    | 12.04     | 43.64     | **A**  |
| P3   | 8,000    | 12.04     | 55.68     | **A**  |
| P8   | 8,000    | 12.04     | 67.72     | **A**  |
| P4   | 7,500    | 11.29     | 79.01     | **B**  |
| P7   | 7,200    | 10.83     | 89.84     | **B**  |
| P6   | 6,750    | 10.16     | 100.00    | **C**  |

**Final Classification:**
- **Class A:** P2, P5, P1, P3, P8 (5 items, 62.5%) → Cumulative value = 67.72%
- **Class B:** P4, P7 (2 items, 25%) → Cumulative value = 22.12%
- **Class C:** P6 (1 item, 12.5%) → Cumulative value = 10.16%

#### Solution to Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Control Policy Recommendations (5 marks):**

| Class | Items | Recommended Control Policy |
|--------|--------|------------------------------|
| **A** | P2, P5, P1, P3, P8 | Strict control: EOQ-based ordering, continuous review system, low safety stock, accurate demand forecasting, frequent physical verification (monthly), detailed record keeping, small frequent orders |
| **B** | P4, P7 | Moderate control: periodic review (quarterly), moderate safety stock, simple trend-based forecasting, medium order size, occasional physical verification |
| **C** | P6 | Loose control: bulk ordering, two-bin system, minimal records, annual or no physical count, large order size to minimize ordering effort |

**Working Capital Reduction Strategy (2 marks):**

By applying differential control, the firm can:
1. **Reduce average inventory** of A-items through tight EOQ optimization — frees working capital.
2. **Reduce safety stock** of C-items through bulk orders and lower service levels — reduces carrying cost.
3. **Reallocate managerial time** to monitor A-items carefully, avoiding overstocking due to uniform policies.

This typically yields a **15–25% reduction in working capital tied up in inventory** without affecting operational efficiency.

---

### Question B — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Sample Paper 2024]**

> A wholesale dealer maintains **9 items** in stock. The Annual Demand and Unit Cost of each item are tabulated below:
>
> | Item | Annual Demand | Unit Cost (₹) |
> |------|----------------|----------------|
> | A1   | 200            | 90             |
> | A2   | 100            | 250            |
> | A3   | 500            | 25             |
> | A4   | 50             | 500            |
> | A5   | 300            | 40             |
> | A6   | 150            | 60             |
> | A7   | 800            | 12             |
> | A8   | 250            | 35             |
> | A9   | 400            | 18             |
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Construct the complete ABC classification using **70%, 90%, and 100%** cumulative value cutoffs. Show all intermediate computations.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the **share of total value and share of total items** for each class. Also compute the **inventory turnover ratio (ITR)** if the total annual sales are ₹1,50,000 and average inventory value is ₹45,000. Comment on the ITR.

#### Solution to Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute ACV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACV}_{A1} &= 200 \times 90 = 18{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{A2} &= 100 \times 250 = 25{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{A3} &= 500 \times 25 = 12{,}500 \\
\text{ACV}_{A4} &= 50 \times 500 = 25{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{A5} &= 300 \times 40 = 12{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{A6} &= 150 \times 60 = 9{,}000 \\
\text{ACV}_{A7} &= 800 \times 12 = 9{,}600 \\
\text{ACV}_{A8} &= 250 \times 35 = 8{,}750 \\
\text{ACV}_{A9} &= 400 \times 18 = 7{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Value**

$$
T = 18000 + 25000 + 12500 + 25000 + 12000 + 9000 + 9600 + 8750 + 7200 = 1{,}27{,}050
$$

**Step 3: Sort in Descending Order and Build Table**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | $p_i$ (%) | $P_k$ (%) | Class |
|------|------|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 1    | A2   | 25,000   | 19.68     | 19.68     | **A**  |
| 2    | A4   | 25,000   | 19.68     | 39.36     | **A**  |
| 3    | A1   | 18,000   | 14.17     | 53.53     | **A**  |
| 4    | A3   | 12,500   | 9.84      | 63.37     | **A**  |
| 5    | A5   | 12,000   | 9.45      | 72.82     | **B**  |
| 6    | A7   | 9,600    | 7.56      | 80.38     | **B**  |
| 7    | A6   | 9,000    | 7.08      | 87.46     | **B**  |
| 8    | A8   | 8,750    | 6.89      | 94.35     | **C**  |
| 9    | A9   | 7,200    | 5.66      | 100.00    | **C**  |

**Final Classification:**
- **A-Class:** A2, A4, A1, A3 (4 items)
- **B-Class:** A5, A7, A6 (3 items)
- **C-Class:** A8, A9 (2 items)

#### Solution to Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Shares Computation (5 marks):**

| Class | Items | Item Share | Value Share (Sum of $p_i$) |
|--------|--------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **A** | 4 of 9 | 44.44% | $19.68 + 19.68 + 14.17 + 9.84 = 63.37\%$ |
| **B** | 3 of 9 | 33.33% | $9.45 + 7.56 + 7.08 = 24.09\%$ |
| **C** | 2 of 9 | 22.22% | $6.89 + 5.66 = 12.55\%$ |

**Verification:** $63.37 + 24.09 + 12.55 = 100.01\% \approx 100\%$ ✓

**Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR) Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ITR} = \frac{\text{Annual Sales}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{45{,}000} = 3.33
$$

> **Comment (additional credit):** An ITR of 3.33 means the firm cycles its inventory approximately **3.33 times per year**, or roughly every **110 days**. This is a low turnover ratio — typical healthy wholesale operations target 6–12. The firm should consider accelerating the turnover of slow-moving C-class items (A8, A9) and tightening A-class reorder cycles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU ABC Analysis Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to sort in descending order (–2 marks):** Many students compute ACV correctly but skip the descending sort. The cumulative percentage is meaningless without correct ordering.
> 2. **Using wrong cutoffs (–1 mark):** KTU may sometimes specify 60/85/100 or 80/95/100. **Always re-read the question for the exact cutoffs** before classifying. Default to 70/90/100 only if no cutoff is mentioned.
> 3. **Conflating Annual Demand with Annual Consumption Value (–2 marks):** A high-demand low-cost item (like I8 in our example) can still be C-class. Always multiply by unit cost.
> 4. **Omitting the cumulative percentage column (–1 mark):** This column is **mandatory** in KTU answer sheets. The examiner needs to see the running cumulative to award full classification marks.
> 5. **Rounding cumulative percentage to the wrong boundary (–1 mark):** For example, classifying an item at $P_k = 70.05\%$ as Class A. KTU follows the **strict inequality rule** — A-class is $P_k \le 70\%$.
> 6. **Skipping control policy recommendations (–3 to –5 marks):** In 14-mark questions, the **control policy part carries equal weight** to the computational part. A perfect ABC table with no control policy discussion can fetch only 7–8 marks.
> 7. **Arithmetic errors in percentage calculation (–1 mark per item):** Always cross-check by ensuring all $p_i$ add to 100% and all $P_k$ end at 100%.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ABC Analysis**

- **ABC** = **Always Better Control** = selective inventory management based on **Pareto's 80/20 rule**.
- The **driving metric** is **Annual Consumption Value (ACV) = Annual Demand × Unit Cost**.
- Items are **sorted in descending order of ACV**, then **cumulative percentage** is calculated progressively.
- **Standard KTU cutoffs (default):**
  - **A-Class:** Cumulative ≤ **70%**
  - **B-Class:** 70% < Cumulative ≤ **90%**
  - **C-Class:** Cumulative > **90%**
- A-class items are **few in number but high in value**; C-class items are **many in number but low in value**.
- **Control policy for A:** Tight, EOQ-based, frequent review, low safety stock, accurate forecasting.
- **Control policy for B:** Moderate, periodic review, medium safety stock.
- **Control policy for C:** Loose, two-bin system, bulk ordering, minimal record keeping.
- **Volume is not value:** A high-demand item can be C-class if its unit cost is low.
- The total of all $p_i$ must equal **100%** (sanity check for arithmetic).
- ABC analysis does **not directly tell us the economic order quantity**; it only classifies items so that **EOQ, ROP, and safety stock can be applied differentially**.
- The number of items in each class satisfies: $n_A + n_B + n_C = n$ (total items).
- Typical industry heuristic (not a rigid law): A-class ≈ 10–20% of items / 70–80% of value; B-class ≈ 20–30% / 15–25%; C-class ≈ 50–70% / 5–10%.
- ABC analysis is a **preliminary classification tool** — it must be followed up with quantitative inventory models (EOQ, ROP) for actual decision-making.
- The classification is **dynamic**: if demand or price changes, the classification must be re-evaluated **annually or quarterly**.
- For KTU answers, always present the solution as a **single master table** with columns: Item, ACV, $p_i$, $P_k$, Class.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of inventory costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT504 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of inventory costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Inventory Control and Management** within Operations Research, **inventory costs** refer to the aggregate monetary expenditures associated with procuring, storing, maintaining, and replenishing stock over a defined planning horizon. These costs form the **objective function** that inventory models (such as the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** family of models) seek to minimize in order to determine the optimal ordering policy for a firm.

The total inventory cost function is generally expressed as a sum of four principal cost components:

$$TC(Q) \;=\; \text{Ordering Cost} \;+\; \text{Carrying Cost} \;+\; \text{Purchase Cost} \;+\; \text{Shortage Cost}$$

where **Q** represents the order quantity per cycle. Each component behaves differently with respect to Q — some are decreasing functions, others are increasing — and their interplay produces the characteristic U-shaped total cost curve that lies at the heart of classical inventory theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 3):** The concept of inventory costs is a *prerequisite foundation* that must be mastered before proceeding to deterministic inventory models (EOQ, EBQ, Production Lot Size, Quantity Discounts) and probabilistic inventory models covered in this module. A weak grasp of cost behavior leads to incorrect EOQ derivations in the exam.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you run a small **kirana (grocery) shop** in Kerala. Every morning you must decide how many rice bags to stock for the week. If you order **too frequently** in small quantities, the delivery boy charges you for every trip → this is your **Ordering Cost**. If you order **too much** and stack rice bags in the back room, you pay rent for that space, the bags may spoil during monsoon, and your money is locked up in unsold stock → this is your **Carrying Cost**. If a customer demands 50 kg of rice and you only have 30 kg, you either lose the sale forever or promise to deliver later, angering the customer → this is your **Shortage Cost**.

The shopkeeper's dilemma is universal: **trade off frequent small orders vs. bulk stockholding vs. risk of running out**. Operations Research quantifies this dilemma with mathematical functions so an optimal middle path can be computed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering parallel:** A manufacturing firm deciding how much raw steel to keep, a hospital pharmacy stocking vaccines, a blood bank maintaining plasma units, and an e-commerce warehouse holding festival inventory — all of them face the *exact same* four-cost trade-off, just at different scales.

## 1.3 Why Inventory Costs Matter in OR

Inventory represents **tied-up capital**. As of 2024 estimates, Indian manufacturing firms hold roughly **3–8% of their annual revenue in inventory**. Reducing this by even 1% through optimal inventory policies can free up crores of rupees. Hence, *understanding the cost structure is the first step toward computing the optimal policy*.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inventory Cost

### 2.1.1 Ordering Cost (Setup Cost), denoted **C₀** or **S**

- Incurred **once per order placed**, regardless of order size (in the basic EOQ model).
- **Direct components:** purchase requisition processing, supplier communication, inspection of incoming goods, transportation/shipping, documentation.
- **Indirect components:** administrative overhead of the purchase department, salary allocation, IT system usage.
- **Mathematical behavior:** *Inversely proportional* to order quantity. Larger Q → fewer orders per year → lower total ordering cost.

### 2.1.2 Carrying Cost (Holding Cost), denoted **C_h** or **H**

- Incurred **per unit of inventory held per unit time** (typically per year).
- **Components:** warehouse rent, utilities, insurance, security, depreciation, taxes, obsolescence, deterioration, and — most importantly — the **opportunity cost of capital** locked in stock.
- Often expressed as a percentage **i** of the unit purchase price **C**: $C_h = i \cdot C$, where $i$ typically ranges from **15% to 25%** per annum in industrial practice.
- **Mathematical behavior:** *Directly proportional* to average inventory, hence to order quantity.

### 2.1.3 Purchase Cost (Material/Acquisition Cost), denoted **C** or **P**

- The **direct cost paid to the supplier per unit** of the item.
- For models with constant unit price, this becomes a **fixed total** over the planning horizon (annual demand × unit price) and **drops out of the optimization** because it is independent of Q. KTU examiners explicitly test whether students recognize this.
- It becomes decision-relevant in **Quantity Discount models** (All-Units Discount, Incremental Discount) where unit price **C** is a step function of Q.

### 2.1.4 Shortage Cost (Stockout Cost), denoted **C_s** or **π**

- Incurred when **demand exceeds available stock** during a cycle.
- Sub-types:
  - **Backordering Cost:** penalty for delayed delivery, often a fixed cost per unit short plus a variable cost proportional to waiting time.
  - **Lost Sales Cost:** profit margin on the lost unit plus intangible loss of customer goodwill.
  - **Lost Goodwill Cost:** long-term reputational damage, often modelled as a lump-sum penalty.
- **Mathematical behavior:** *Inversely related* to inventory safety level; increases as Q decreases (in the basic model) or as safety stock decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 distinction:** Module 3 covers **both** deterministic models (no shortage, with shortage, with quantity discounts, production lot size, instantaneous and finite replenishment) **and** stochastic models. The cost structure adapts: in shortage models, $C_s$ enters the objective; in basic EOQ, $C_s \to \infty$ (i.e., stockouts are *not permitted*).

## 2.2 Cost Behavior with Respect to Order Quantity (Q)

| Cost Component | Functional Form (per year) | Behavior as Q ↑ | Behavior as Q ↓ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Ordering Cost** | $TOC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot C_0$ | Decreases | Increases |
| **Total Carrying Cost** | $TCC(Q) = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$ | Increases | Decreases |
| **Total Purchase Cost** | $TPC = D \cdot C$ | Constant (no discount) | Constant |
| **Total Shortage Cost** | $TSC(Q)$ (model-dependent) | Decreases | Increases |
| **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** | $TVC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} C_0 + \dfrac{Q}{2} C_h$ | U-shaped | U-shaped |

where:
- $D$ = annual demand (units/year), assumed deterministic and constant
- $C_0$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $C_h$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $C$ = unit purchase price (₹/unit)
- $Q$ = order quantity per cycle (units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The **Purchase Cost** is independent of Q in the basic EOQ model. KTU examiners frequently award a 2-mark question asking *"Why is purchase cost ignored while deriving EOQ?"* The answer: because it is a *constant* for given D and C, and adding a constant to a function does not change its optimal Q. Mathematically, $\dfrac{d}{dQ}(D \cdot C) = 0$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Units | Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $EOQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_0}{C_h}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | units | No shortage, instant replenishment |
| 2 | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_0}{D \, C_h}}$ | Optimal cycle time | years | Same as above |
| 3 | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \, C_h}{2 C_0}}$ | Optimal number of orders/yr | orders/yr | Same as above |
| 4 | $TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h}$ | Minimum total variable cost | ₹/yr | Basic EOQ at optimum |
| 5 | $C_h = i \cdot C$ | Holding cost from carrying % | ₹/unit/yr | When carrying cost rate **i** is given |
| 6 | $AOC = C_0 \cdot N = \dfrac{D}{Q} C_0$ | Annual Ordering Cost | ₹/yr | Basic EOQ |
| 7 | $ACC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$ | Annual Carrying Cost | ₹/yr | Basic EOQ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{AOC}{ACC} = 1$ at $Q^*$ | Cost-balance property | dimensionless | Fundamental EOQ property |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supply Chain Management:** Firms like Flipkart, Amazon, and HUL compute EOQ for thousands of SKUs to determine warehouse replenishment.
- **Healthcare Operations:** Blood banks and vaccine cold chains use EOQ variants where $C_0$ is the procurement administrative cost and $C_h$ includes refrigeration energy.
- **Manufacturing:** A steel plant orders coal; optimal lot size balances freight ($C_0$) vs. coal yard storage ($C_h$).
- **Defense & Aerospace:** Spare-parts inventory for aircraft is governed by EOQ with high $C_s$ (mission failure is catastrophic), pushing Q* upward and toward higher service levels.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Variable Cost Function

We model a deterministic, instantaneous-replenishment inventory system with **uniform annual demand D** and **constant order quantity Q** per cycle.

**Step 1 — Number of orders per year:**

$$\text{Number of orders} = \frac{\text{Annual demand}}{\text{Order quantity}} = \frac{D}{Q}$$

Each order is placed every $T$ time units, where the cycle time is $T = Q/D$ years.

**Step 2 — Annual Ordering Cost (AOC):**

Since each order costs $C_0$ and there are $D/Q$ orders per year:

$$AOC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_0$$

**Step 3 — Average Inventory:**

For a system where inventory is consumed at a constant rate starting at Q and reaching 0 at the end of the cycle, the inventory level drops **linearly**. The average inventory is the area under the sawtooth divided by cycle length, which geometrically equals the midpoint:

$$\text{Average inventory} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Carrying Cost (ACC):**

Multiplying average inventory by the per-unit holding cost $C_h$:

$$ACC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$$

**Step 5 — Total Variable Cost (excluding the constant purchase cost):**

$$\boxed{TVC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \, C_0 \;+\; \frac{Q}{2} \, C_h}$$

**Step 6 — Intuition Check:** When $Q$ is very small, $D/Q$ is huge → ordering cost dominates. When $Q$ is very large, $Q/2$ is huge → carrying cost dominates. There must exist a unique Q* minimizing TVC — this is the U-shaped curve seen in every KTU EOQ problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

To find the Q that minimizes $TVC(Q)$, we take the first derivative and set it to zero.

**Step 1 — Differentiate TVC with respect to Q:**

$$\frac{d \, TVC}{dQ} = -\frac{D \, C_0}{Q^2} \;+\; \frac{C_h}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to zero (first-order necessary condition):**

$$-\frac{D \, C_0}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$\frac{C_h}{2} = \frac{D \, C_0}{Q^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^2 = \frac{2 D C_0}{C_h}$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive square root (since Q > 0):**

$$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_h}}}$$

**Step 5 — Second-derivative test for minimum:**

$$\frac{d^2 TVC}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D C_0}{Q^3} \;>\; 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**, confirming the U-shape.

**Step 6 — Cost-balance property at optimum (KTU favourite 2-mark sub-question):**

At $Q = Q^*$:

$$AOC(Q^*) = \frac{D}{Q^*} C_0 = \frac{D \, C_0}{\sqrt{2 D C_0 / C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \, C_0 \, C_h}{2}}$$

$$ACC(Q^*) = \frac{Q^*}{2} C_h = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_0}{C_h}} \cdot C_h = \sqrt{\frac{D \, C_0 \, C_h}{2}}$$

Hence $AOC(Q^*) = ACC(Q^*)$. **At the optimal order quantity, the annual ordering cost exactly equals the annual carrying cost** — a beautiful and useful diagnostic property.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Minimum Total Variable Cost

Substituting $Q^*$ back into $TVC$:

$$TVC_{\min} = \frac{D}{Q^*} C_0 + \frac{Q^*}{2} C_h = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D \, C_0 \, C_h}{2}} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h}$$

Equivalently, $TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h}$. This closed-form result lets the examiner skip intermediate steps and award full marks for the final number in numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A firm uses **24,000 units/year** of a raw material. Each order incurs an ordering cost of **₹150**. The unit price is **₹20** and the carrying cost is **20% per annum** of the unit price. Lead time is 1 week (52-week year). Compute (a) EOQ, (b) optimal number of orders per year, (c) minimum total variable cost, (d) reorder point.

**Given:** $D = 24{,}000$ units/yr, $C_0 = ₹150$/order, $C = ₹20$/unit, $i = 20\% = 0.20$, hence $C_h = 0.20 \times 20 = ₹4$/unit/yr.

**Solution:**

**(a) EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000} = 1341.64 \text{ units}$$

**(b) Optimal number of orders per year:**

$$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1341.64} \approx 17.89 \approx 18 \text{ orders/yr}$$

**(c) Minimum total variable cost:**

$$TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 4} = \sqrt{28{,}800{,}000} = ₹5{,}366.56/\text{yr}$$

Cross-check: $AOC = 17.89 \times 150 = ₹2{,}683.5$; $ACC = (1341.64/2) \times 4 = ₹2{,}683.3$ → sum ≈ ₹5,366.8 ✓ (matches cost-balance property).

**(d) Reorder Point (ROP):**

$$R = d \times L = \frac{24{,}000}{52} \times 1 = 461.54 \approx 462 \text{ units}$$

where $d = D/\text{working weeks}$ is weekly demand and $L$ is lead time in weeks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Cost Models in Module 3

| Model | Ordering Cost Term | Carrying Cost Term | Shortage Cost Term | Purchase Cost Treatment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basic EOQ (no shortage)** | $D C_0 / Q$ | $Q C_h / 2$ | Absent (infinite) | Constant — dropped |
| **EOQ with shortages (planned)** | $D C_0 / Q$ | $C_h (Q - S)^2 / (2 Q)$ | $C_s S^2 / (2 Q)$ | Constant — dropped |
| **Production Lot Size (EPQ)** | $D C_0 / Q$ | $C_h Q (1 - D/p) / 2$ | Absent | Constant — dropped |
| **Quantity Discount Model** | $D C_0 / Q$ | $Q C_h / 2$ | Absent | $D \cdot C(Q)$ — *decision-relevant* |
| **Probabilistic Model** | $D C_0 / Q$ | $C_h [Q/2 + \text{SS}]$ | $C_s \cdot f(\text{stockouts})$ | Constant |

Notation key: $S$ = backorder level, $p$ = production rate, $\text{SS}$ = safety stock, $f(\cdot)$ = expected stockout function.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Notice how the **carrying cost term changes form** when shortages are allowed — the average inventory is no longer $Q/2$ but $(Q - S)^2 / (2Q)$ because part of the cycle has zero inventory. Students who blindly apply $Q/2$ in shortage models will lose 4–5 marks instantly.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Ordering cost, (ii) Carrying cost, (iii) Shortage cost.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ordering Cost (C₀):** The fixed expenses incurred each time a purchase order is placed with a supplier, independent of the order size. *Example:* A retailer pays a fixed ₹500 per delivery trip for paperwork, transport, and inspection.
2. **Carrying Cost (C_h):** The cost of holding one unit of inventory in storage for one year, expressed per unit per year. It includes rent, insurance, deterioration, and opportunity cost of locked capital. *Example:* A godown charges ₹40 per unit per year for storing cement bags.
3. **Shortage Cost (C_s):** The penalty incurred when customer demand cannot be met from current stock. *Example:* A pharmaceutical company losing ₹200 of profit per unit of insulin not available, plus goodwill loss.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> *Why is the purchase cost ignored while deriving the EOQ in the classical inventory model? State the cost-balance property at optimum.*

**Model Answer:**

Purchase cost $D \cdot C$ is **independent of the order quantity Q** in the basic EOQ model (no quantity discount). Since adding a constant to the objective function $TVC(Q)$ does not change its optimal Q, the term drops out during differentiation: $\dfrac{d}{dQ}(D \cdot C) = 0$.

**Cost-Balance Property:** At the optimal order quantity $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 / C_h}$, the annual ordering cost exactly equals the annual carrying cost, i.e., $\dfrac{D C_0}{Q^*} = \dfrac{Q^* C_h}{2}$. This is a useful diagnostic — if the firm observes that one cost is much larger than the other, Q is not optimal.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model] — 14 Marks**
> *A manufacturing company requires 18,000 units/year of a component. The ordering cost is ₹200 per order, the unit cost is ₹50, and the inventory carrying cost is 18% per year of the unit cost. Lead time is 15 days (assume 360-day year).*
>
> **(a)** Determine the Economic Order Quantity, the optimal number of orders per year, the time between orders, and the minimum total variable cost. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Determine the reorder point. If the company currently orders 2,000 units at a time, calculate the extra cost the company is incurring compared to the EOQ policy. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution (A)

**Given:** $D = 18{,}000$ units/yr, $C_0 = ₹200$/order, $C = ₹50$/unit, $i = 0.18$, hence $C_h = 0.18 \times 50 = ₹9$/unit/yr, Lead time $L = 15$ days.

#### (a) EOQ and Related Parameters

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200}{9}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{9}} = \sqrt{800{,}000} \approx 894.43 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{18{,}000}{894.43} \approx 20.12 \approx 21 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 3 — Time between consecutive orders:**

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{894.43}{18{,}000} \text{ yr} \approx 0.0497 \text{ yr} \approx 18.15 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum total variable cost:**

$$TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200 \times 9} = \sqrt{64{,}800{,}000} = ₹8{,}050.3/\text{year}$$

Cross-check: $AOC = (D/Q^*) C_0 = 20.12 \times 200 = ₹4{,}024.7$; $ACC = (Q^*/2) C_h = (894.43/2) \times 9 = ₹4{,}024.9$ ✓.

#### (b) Reorder Point and Extra Cost of Suboptimal Q = 2000

**Step 1 — Reorder Point:**

$$R = d \times L = \frac{18{,}000}{360} \times 15 = 50 \times 15 = 750 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Total variable cost at Q = 2000:**

$$TVC(2000) = \frac{18{,}000}{2000} \times 200 + \frac{2000}{2} \times 9 = 9 \times 200 + 1000 \times 9 = 1800 + 9000 = ₹10{,}800/\text{year}$$

**Step 3 — Extra cost incurred:**

$$\text{Extra Cost} = TVC(2000) - TVC_{\min} = 10{,}800 - 8{,}050.3 = ₹2{,}749.7/\text{year}$$

> **Final Answers (A):** (a) $Q^* \approx 894$ units, $N^* \approx 20$ orders/yr, $T^* \approx 18$ days, $TVC_{\min} \approx ₹8{,}050$/yr. (b) $ROP = 750$ units, Extra cost ≈ ₹2,750/yr.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Model] — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**
> *A company has an annual demand of 36,000 units for a product. The cost of placing one order is ₹400 and the carrying cost is ₹6 per unit per year. The purchase price is ₹100 per unit.*
>
> **(a)** Calculate the EOQ, the number of orders per year, and the minimum total inventory cost per year (including purchase cost). **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** If the supplier offers a 5% discount on the unit price if the order quantity is 4,000 units or more, determine whether the firm should accept the discount. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution (B)

**Given:** $D = 36{,}000$ units/yr, $C_0 = ₹400$/order, $C = ₹100$/unit, $C_h = ₹6$/unit/yr.

#### (a) EOQ, Orders/yr, Minimum Total Cost

**Step 1 — EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{28{,}800{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{4{,}800{,}000} \approx 2{,}190.89 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$N^* = \frac{36{,}000}{2{,}190.89} \approx 16.43 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum total inventory cost (including purchase cost):**

$$TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400 \times 6} = \sqrt{172{,}800{,}000} = ₹13{,}146.4/\text{yr}$$

Adding the (constant) annual purchase cost:

$$TC_{\min} = 13{,}146.4 + (36{,}000 \times 100) = ₹36{,}13{,}146.4/\text{yr} \approx ₹36.13 \text{ lakh/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check at optimum:** $AOC = (36{,}000/2190.89) \times 400 \approx ₹6{,}573.2$; $ACC = (2190.89/2) \times 6 \approx ₹6{,}572.7$ → equal ✓.

#### (b) Discount Analysis (Q_d = 4,000, Discounted Price = ₹95/unit)

**Step 1 — Recompute $C_h$ at discounted price (assuming 20% rate is unchanged, the problem states the holding cost is still ₹6, so we treat $C_h$ as fixed at ₹6):**

Since $C_h = ₹6$ is given absolutely, we do not re-derive it. $C_h$ remains ₹6/unit/yr.

**Step 2 — Total cost at EOQ with new price (Q still = 2190.89):**

$$TC_{EOQ}^{discount} = \frac{36{,}000}{2{,}190.89} \times 400 + \frac{2{,}190.89}{2} \times 6 + 36{,}000 \times 95$$

$$= 6{,}573.2 + 6{,}572.7 + 34{,}20{,}000 = ₹34{,}33{,}145.9/\text{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Total cost at discount break Q = 4,000 with discounted price ₹95:**

$$TC_{4000} = \frac{36{,}000}{4{,}000} \times 400 + \frac{4{,}000}{2} \times 6 + 36{,}000 \times 95$$

$$= 9 \times 400 + 2{,}000 \times 6 + 34{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}600 + 12{,}000 + 34{,}20{,}000 = ₹34{,}35{,}600/\text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$TC_{4000} - TC_{EOQ}^{discount} = 34{,}35{,}600 - 34{,}33{,}145.9 = ₹2{,}454.1/\text{yr}$$

Since $TC_{EOQ}^{discount} < TC_{4000}$, the firm should **not** accept the discount; it should continue ordering at EOQ even with the higher unit price, saving about ₹2,454 per year.

> **Final Answers (B):** (a) $Q^* \approx 2{,}191$ units, $N^* \approx 16.43$ orders/yr, $TC_{\min} \approx ₹36.13$ lakh/yr. (b) Do not accept the discount — savings of ≈ ₹2,454/yr by staying at EOQ.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Lead Time units:** Many students use $L = 15$ days in *years* (15/365) instead of (15/360). Always state your assumption — examiners expect 360-day year unless otherwise noted.
> 2. **Confusing $C_h$ and $i$:** If the problem says "carrying cost is 20%", convert to $C_h = i \cdot C$ *before* using the EOQ formula. Plugging $i = 0.20$ directly into $\sqrt{2 D C_0 / i}$ is a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the cost-balance property in discount analysis:** Always compute *both* $TVC$ at the discount break and at the EOQ with the discounted price. Choosing the larger Q without this check costs 4–5 marks.
> 4. **Off-by-one rounding:** Number of orders must be rounded **up** (you cannot place 17.89 orders), but EOQ itself is rounded to the nearest integer; clearly show both steps.
> 5. **Sign convention in shortage cost derivation:** $S$ (backorder level) is a *positive* quantity measured from 0. Do not confuse with the inventory level $(Q - S)$ which is also positive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] Inventory costs consist of **four components**: Ordering, Carrying, Purchase, and Shortage.
- [x] In basic EOQ: **TOC = D C₀ / Q** (decreases with Q), **TCC = Q C_h / 2** (increases with Q), giving a **U-shaped TVC**.
- [x] **EOQ formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_0 / C_h}$ — derived by setting $\dfrac{d TVC}{dQ} = 0$.
- [x] **Cost-balance property at optimum:** Annual ordering cost = Annual carrying cost. Use this as a sanity check in every numerical problem.
- [x] **Minimum TVC:** $TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D C_0 C_h}$ — closed form, useful for quick verification.
- [x] **Purchase cost is constant** in the basic model and *drops out of optimization*; it becomes decision-relevant **only** in quantity discount models.
- [x] **Carrying cost conversion:** $C_h = i \cdot C$, where **i** is the carrying charge rate (typical industrial range: 15–25% per year).
- [x] **Reorder Point:** $R = d \times L$, where $d$ is daily demand and $L$ is lead time. Always state unit assumptions.
- [x] **Shortage cost** is absent in basic EOQ (treated as infinite); it is introduced in the *Planned Shortage* model where both $Q$ and the backorder level $S$ become decision variables.
- [x] **Production Lot Size (EPQ)** modifies the carrying term to $\dfrac{Q C_h}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{D}{p}\right)$ to account for finite replenishment rate $p > D$.
- [x] **Discount rule:** Always compare $TC$ at EOQ with discounted price vs. $TC$ at break quantity with discounted price — pick the minimum. **Never** blindly pick the larger Q.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of inventory policy, Order
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT504 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of inventory policy, Order

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Inventory** refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization at a given point in time. It represents a **capital resource** that is tied up but is essential to maintain smooth operations of the production–distribution system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Policy (KTU Definition):** A set of rules and decision parameters that govern *when* to place an order (reorder point), *how much* to order (order quantity), and *how* to manage stock levels (continuous vs. periodic review) to minimize the total cost of inventory while meeting customer demand.

## 1.2 Components of an Inventory Policy

An inventory policy is mathematically defined by three fundamental decision variables:

1. **Order Quantity (Q)** — The number of units ordered each time replenishment occurs.
2. **Reorder Point (ROP / r)** — The inventory level at which a new order must be placed.
3. **Review Period (T)** — The fixed interval (for periodic systems) at which stock is checked and orders are placed.

## 1.3 Types of Inventory

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials** | Inputs awaiting processing | Steel coils, electronic chips |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed goods | Assembled sub-assemblies |
| **Finished Goods** | Completed products awaiting sale | Packed laptops in warehouse |
| **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating)** | Supplies for operations | Lubricants, tools, spares |
| **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** | Goods in transit between stages | Shipments in trucks/containers |
| **Safety / Buffer Stock** | Extra units to absorb demand variability | Emergency demand cushion |
| **Anticipation Stock** | Built up for known future demand spikes | Diwali inventory in retail |

## 1.4 Why Organizations Hold Inventory — The 8 Strategic Reasons

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List any four reasons for holding inventory."* Memorize this list.

1. **To meet anticipated customer demand** (decoupling supply from demand).
2. **To decouple production stages** — buffers allow each stage to operate independently.
3. **To protect against stockouts** (lost sales and backorder costs).
4. **To take advantage of quantity discounts** (economic order quantities).
5. **To hedge against price inflation** and supply shortages.
6. **To smooth production** — produce in large lots, sell steadily.
7. **To pipeline-transport goods** (oil in pipelines, goods in transit).
8. **To manage uncertainty** in demand and lead time variability.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Pantry" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine your home kitchen as a small factory.** Your fridge is the inventory. You buy vegetables (raw material), store them (holding cost), cook meals (WIP), and serve dinner (finished good demand).
>
> - If you buy **too much** — vegetables rot (waste = high holding cost).
> - If you buy **too little** — guests go hungry (stockout = lost goodwill).
> - If you buy **too frequently** — you waste time and fuel going to the market (high ordering cost).
> - The *inventory policy* is your personal rule: *"I will go to the market when stocks drop to 2 kg of onions, and I will always buy 5 kg."* — that is your **(s, Q) policy** with s = 2, Q = 5.

The fundamental goal of any inventory system is to find the **golden balance** between three competing cost components.

## 1.6 Real-World Engineering Examples

- **Manufacturing Plant:** A car factory stocks 10,000 brake discs to avoid line stoppage.
- **Hospital Pharmacy:** Stocks life-saving drugs — **service level** must be near **100%**.
- **IT Data Center:** Spare servers kept as buffer stock against hardware failure.
- **Civil Engineering:** Cement and steel stock maintained at site against monsoon disruption.
- **Retail Store:** Wal-Mart's famous cross-docking model *minimizes* inventory via frequent replenishment.

## 1.7 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold for Recall)

- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — the optimal order size minimizing total cost.
- **Reorder Point (ROP)** — the trigger level for placing a new order.
- **Lead Time (L)** — gap between order placement and order receipt.
- **Safety Stock (SS)** — buffer above ROP for demand uncertainty.
- **Service Level (β)** — probability of not stocking out (commonly **95%** or **97.5%**).
- **Holding Cost Rate (i)** — typically **20% to 40%** of item value per year.
- **Number of Orders per Year (N)** — inverse of cycle time.
- **Total Relevant Cost (TC)** — sum of ordering + holding + shortage costs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Cost Components of Inventory

Every inventory decision in Operations Research is governed by the trade-off between **three cost categories**. This is the single most important conceptual foundation in this module.

### 2.1.1 Ordering Cost (also called Setup Cost or Procurement Cost)

**Definition:** The fixed cost incurred *each time* an order is placed, *independent* of the order size.

Components include:
- Purchase requisition and purchase order processing
- Transportation and shipping (inbound logistics)
- Inspection and receiving
- Administrative paperwork
- Machine setup cost (in production)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Ordering cost is a **FIXED cost per order** and is therefore a *step function* — it does not change with Q. Total annual ordering cost = (Number of orders) × (Cost per order).

### 2.1.2 Holding (Carrying) Cost

**Definition:** The cost of keeping one unit of inventory in storage for a unit of time (usually one year).

Components include:
- **Capital cost** (opportunity cost of money tied up) — typically the largest component
- **Storage cost** (warehouse rent, utilities, insurance)
- **Obsolescence cost** (perishability, expiry, fashion changes)
- **Shrinkage cost** (theft, damage, evaporation)
- **Insurance and taxes**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holding cost is proportional to the AVERAGE inventory level.** When you order Q units, you carry on average Q/2 units, because inventory linearly declines from Q to 0 before the next order arrives.

### 2.1.3 Shortage (Stockout) Cost

**Definition:** The penalty incurred when demand cannot be met from stock.

Two policy responses:
- **Backordering (Backlog):** Customer waits; cost = lost goodwill + delayed revenue.
- **Lost Sales:** Customer leaves; cost = direct revenue loss + permanent goodwill loss.

## 2.2 Visualizing the Cost Trade-off — The Fundamental Tension

| Order Quantity (Q) | Ordering Cost (OC) | Holding Cost (HC) | Total Cost Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Small Q** | ↑ High (many orders) | ↓ Low (low average stock) | Sub-optimal |
| **Large Q** | ↓ Low (few orders) | ↑ High (high average stock) | Sub-optimal |
| **Optimal Q = EOQ** | Equal to HC | Equal to HC | **MINIMUM** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight (The EOQ Theorem):** At the optimal order quantity, **Annual Ordering Cost = Annual Holding Cost**. This is the KTU examiner's favorite check question — always verify this equality in your optimal solution.

## 2.3 The Order Quantity Decision — Demand Patterns

### 2.3.1 Deterministic vs. Stochastic Demand

- **Deterministic (Known, Constant):** Demand rate D is fixed per unit time. (Basic EOQ model)
- **Stochastic (Probabilistic):** Demand follows a probability distribution. (Newsvendor, (s, Q) policy with safety stock)

### 2.3.2 Replenishment Patterns

- **Instantaneous (Infinite) Replenishment:** Order arrives in one batch at a single instant. (Standard EOQ assumption)
- **Finite Replenishment (Production Lot):** Items are produced or supplied at a finite rate *p* (Production Order Quantity / EPQ model).

## 2.4 Inventory Control Systems — Two Fundamental Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 explicitly tests these two systems. Be ready to write short notes on each.

### 2.4.1 Continuous Review System — The (Q, r) or (s, Q) Model

- Stock level is monitored **continuously** (every transaction).
- When inventory position **drops to reorder point r**, a fixed order of size **Q** is placed.
- **Advantage:** Lower safety stock requirement; reacts immediately to demand.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires real-time tracking (expensive for low-value items).
- **Used for:** High-value items (e.g., gold, pharmaceuticals, server spares).

### 2.4.2 Periodic Review System — The (R, S) or (s, S) Model

- Stock is reviewed **periodically** at fixed intervals of length **R**.
- At each review, inventory is **topped up** to level **S**.
- Order quantity = (S − current inventory level).
- **Advantage:** Simpler to administer; convenient for joint ordering.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires higher safety stock (variance accumulates over R).
- **Used for:** Grouped items, retail stores with weekly stock checks.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All these formulas appear verbatim in the KTU Module 3 syllabus. The last column gives the unit; KTU sometimes tests unit consistency.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Annual demand (given) | Total units required per year | units/yr |
| $Q$ | Order quantity | Units per order | units/order |
| $N$ | $N = D / Q$ | Number of orders per year | orders/yr |
| $T$ | $T = Q / D$ | Cycle time (length of one cycle) | years |
| $K$ or $C_o$ | Fixed ordering cost | Cost per order placed | ₹/order |
| $H$ or $C_h$ | $H = i \cdot C$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit·yr |
| $i$ | $0.20 \text{ to } 0.40$ | Annual holding charge (fraction of unit cost) | dimensionless |
| $C$ | — | Purchase cost per unit | ₹/unit |
| $L$ | Lead time | Order placement to receipt | days or weeks |
| $r$ | $r = D \cdot L$ | Reorder point (deterministic) | units |
| $SS$ | $z \cdot \sigma_{dLT}$ | Safety stock | units |
| $\beta$ | $0.90, 0.95, 0.975$ | Service level (cycle / fill rate) | dimensionless |
| $TC$ | $TC = \frac{D}{Q}K + \frac{Q}{2}H$ | Total relevant annual cost (EOQ) | ₹/yr |

## 2.6 Applications in Engineering Practice

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Spare parts inventory for CNC machines — avoid line stoppage.
- **Civil Engineering:** Steel and cement stock for continuous project execution.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Inventory of rare components (IGBTs, ICs) for control panel production.
- **Industrial Engineering:** JIT (Just-In-Time) inventory systems in automotive assembly.
- **Data Engineering:** Buffer caches in distributed systems (conceptually identical to safety stock).

## 2.7 Common KTU Conceptual Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Don't confuse Total Cost (TC) with Unit Cost (C).** TC includes C × D only if explicitly asked; basic EOQ ignores the purchase cost as it is constant w.r.t. Q.
> 2. **Don't forget the average inventory is Q/2**, not Q.
> 3. **Reorder point depends on LEAD TIME, not cycle time.** ROP = D × L.
> 4. **Safety stock depends on DEMAND VARIABILITY during lead time**, not annual demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic EOQ Formula (Wilson's Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **core analytical result** of Module 3. The KTU board examiner's valuation key requires every algebraic step below. **Do not skip steps.**

### 3.1.1 Set-Up of the Model — Assumptions

We begin by stating the classical assumptions of the basic deterministic EOQ model:

1. Demand rate **D** is **known and constant** (deterministic, continuous drawdown).
2. Replenishment is **instantaneous** — the entire order of size Q arrives in a single batch at one moment in time.
3. **Lead time L is known and constant.**
4. **Ordering cost K** is fixed per order, independent of Q.
5. **Holding cost H per unit per year** is constant and proportional to average inventory.
6. **No stockouts are permitted** — every demand is immediately met from stock.
7. **Order quantity Q is the sole decision variable.**

### 3.1.2 Expression for Annual Ordering Cost

**Logical reasoning:** If annual demand is D units and each order brings in Q units, then the **number of orders per year** is N = D / Q. Since each order costs K rupees, the total annual ordering cost is:

$$
OC = \left(\frac{\text{Number of orders}}{\text{year}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Cost per order}}\right) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot K
$$

**Step 1 — Total annual ordering cost:**

$$
OC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot K}{Q}
$$

### 3.1.3 Expression for Annual Holding Cost

**Logical reasoning:** Inventory starts at level Q immediately after an order is received, then declines at a constant rate (rate = D per year) until it reaches zero just as the next order arrives. The **average inventory** over one cycle is the arithmetic mean of the maximum (Q) and minimum (0) levels:

$$
\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Total annual holding cost:**

$$
HC(Q) = \text{Average Inventory} \times \text{Holding cost per unit per year} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

### 3.1.4 Total Relevant Annual Cost Function

**Step 3 — Sum the two cost components** (the constant purchase cost C·D is omitted as it is independent of Q and does not affect the optimal Q):

$$
TC(Q) = OC(Q) + HC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot K}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
$$

This is the famous **Total Cost Curve** in inventory theory.

### 3.1.5 Optimality Condition — First-Order Necessary Condition

**Step 4 — Differentiate TC(Q) with respect to Q** and set the derivative equal to zero to find the stationary point:

$$
\frac{d \, TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot K}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the first-order necessary condition** for a minimum (set derivative = 0):

$$
-\frac{D \cdot K}{Q^{*\,2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### 3.1.6 Solving for the Optimum

**Step 6 — Rearrange the equation** by moving the first term to the right-hand side:

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot K}{Q^{*\,2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by Q\*²:**

$$
Q^{*\,2} \cdot \frac{H}{2} = D \cdot K
$$

**Step 8 — Isolate Q\*²:**

$$
Q^{*\,2} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot K}{H}
$$

**Step 9 — Take the positive square root** (since order quantity is non-negative):

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot K}{H}}
$$

This is the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, also called **Wilson's Lot-Size Formula** (Ford W. Harris, 1913).

### 3.1.7 Second-Order Sufficiency Check (Concavity Verification)

**Step 10 — Compute the second derivative:**

$$
\frac{d^{2}\, TC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot K}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is strictly positive, TC(Q) is **strictly convex** in Q, confirming the stationary point is a **global minimum**.

### 3.1.8 The Cost-Equality Property at EOQ

**Step 11 — Substitute Q\* into the cost equations:**

$$
OC(Q^{*}) = \frac{D \cdot K}{Q^{*}} = \frac{D \cdot K}{\sqrt{2 D K / H}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot K \cdot H}{2}}
$$

$$
HC(Q^{*}) = \frac{Q^{*} \cdot H}{2} = \frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot K \cdot H}{2}}
$$

**Conclusion:** $OC(Q^{*}) = HC(Q^{*}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot K \cdot H}{2}}$. This proves the cost-equality theorem.

### 3.1.9 Minimum Total Cost Formula

**Step 12 — Substitute back into TC:**

$$
TC_{\min} = OC(Q^{*}) + HC(Q^{*}) = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot K \cdot H}{2}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot K \cdot H}
$$

The **minimum total relevant annual cost** is:

$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot K \cdot H}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cost-Behavior Summary

| Quantity (Q) | OC = DK/Q | HC = QH/2 | TC | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Q \to 0$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to 0$ | $\to \infty$ | Ordering dominates |
| $Q = Q^*/2$ | $2 \cdot \sqrt{DKH/2}$ | $\sqrt{DKH/2}/2$ | $>TC_{min}$ | Convex region |
| $Q = Q^*$ | $\sqrt{DKH/2}$ | $\sqrt{DKH/2}$ | $\sqrt{2DKH}$ | **Global minimum** |
| $Q = 2Q^*$ | $\sqrt{DKH/2}/2$ | $2 \cdot \sqrt{DKH/2}$ | $>TC_{min}$ | Holding dominates |
| $Q \to \infty$ | $\to 0$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ | Holding dominates |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A manufacturing firm uses 24,000 units/year of a component. The ordering cost is ₹150 per order. The holding cost is ₹4 per unit per year. Determine: (a) EOQ, (b) Number of orders per year, (c) Cycle time, (d) Reorder point if lead time is 7 days, (e) Total annual cost, and (f) Verify OC = HC at optimum.

**Given:** D = 24,000 units/yr, K = ₹150/order, H = ₹4/unit·yr, L = 7 days.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot K}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{4}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000} = 1341.64 \approx 1342 \text{ units}
$$

**Step (b) — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{24{,}000}{1342} = 17.88 \approx 18 \text{ orders per year}
$$

**Step (c) — Cycle time (assuming 365 operating days/year):**

$$
T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1342}{24{,}000} \text{ yr} = 0.0559 \text{ yr} = 20.4 \text{ days}
$$

**Step (d) — Reorder point (ROP):**

$$
r = D \cdot L = \left(\frac{24{,}000}{365}\right) \times 7 = 65.75 \times 7 = 460.27 \approx 461 \text{ units}
$$

**Step (e) — Total annual cost:**

$$
TC = \sqrt{2 D K H} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 4} = \sqrt{28{,}800{,}000} = ₹5{,}366.56
$$

**Step (f) — Verify OC = HC at Q\*:**

$$
OC = \frac{D \cdot K}{Q^{*}} = \frac{24{,}000 \times 150}{1342} = ₹2{,}682.56
$$

$$
HC = \frac{Q^{*} \cdot H}{2} = \frac{1342 \times 4}{2} = ₹2{,}684.00
$$

The small discrepancy (₹2) is due to rounding Q* to 1342. The exact equality at the unrounded Q* is mathematically guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time (Saw-Tooth Pattern)

The classic **saw-tooth diagram** is the visual signature of a basic EOQ system. Each cycle begins with a vertical jump (instantaneous replenishment) and ends with a linear decline to zero (constant demand).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CYCLE START: Order Q received] --> B[Inventory at level Q]
    B --> C[Inventory declines at rate D per year]
    C --> D[Inventory hits zero: Reorder Point r reached]
    D --> E[New order placed: Lead time L starts]
    E --> F[After L time units, new Q arrives]
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Between t = 0 and t = T (cycle time), inventory drops from Q to 0. The next order is triggered when the on-hand stock reaches r. The order arrives Q units later, restarting the cycle.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Inventory Policy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Demand Stream D] --> B[On-Hand Inventory Monitor]
    B --> C{Inventory less than r?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Place Order for Q units]
    C -->|No| E[Continue Sales / Issue]
    D --> F[Supplier Lead Time L]
    F --> G[Order Receipt: Add Q to Stock]
    G --> B
    E --> B
    B --> H[Compute Holding Cost H per cycle]
    D --> I[Compute Ordering Cost K per order]
    H --> J[Total Relevant Cost TC]
    I --> J
```

> [!TIP]
> **This functional block diagram is exactly what the KTU 2024 scheme expects** when a question asks: *"Draw the flow chart of an inventory control system."* Memorize the core decision diamond: **{Inventory < r ?}**.

## 4.3 Cost Trade-Off Curve — Visual Intuition

```mermaid
graph LR
    OC[Annual Ordering Cost: D times K over Q] --> DEC[Decreasing Convex Curve]
    HC[Annual Holding Cost: Q times H over 2] --> INC[Increasing Convex Curve]
    DEC --> SUM
    INC --> SUM
    SUM[Sum TC: U-shaped convex curve] --> MIN[Minimum at Q star]
    MIN --> EQ[OC equals HC at optimum]
```

## 4.4 Continuous Review (Q,r) vs. Periodic Review (R,S) — Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CR[CONTINUOUS REVIEW Q r SYSTEM]
        CR1[Monitor stock every transaction] --> CR2{Stock drops to r?}
        CR2 -->|Yes| CR3[Order fixed Q units]
        CR2 -->|No| CR1
    end
    subgraph PR[PERIODIC REVIEW R S SYSTEM]
        PR1[Wait for period R] --> PR2[Check current stock I]
        PR2 --> PR3[Order quantity equals S minus I]
        PR3 --> PR1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** Don't confuse the two systems. Continuous review (Q, r) uses a **fixed quantity** with a **variable order interval**. Periodic review (R, S) uses a **fixed interval** with a **variable order quantity**.

## 4.5 ABC Inventory Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[All Inventory Items] --> B[Class A: 10 to 20 percent items, 70 to 80 percent value]
    A --> C[Class B: 30 percent items, 15 percent value]
    A --> D[Class C: 50 percent items, 5 to 10 percent value]
    B --> E[Tight control, frequent review, EOQ]
    C --> F[Moderate control, periodic review]
    D --> G[Loose control, bulk ordering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Pareto principle (80/20 rule)** applied to inventory — a favorite KTU short-answer topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define inventory and list any four reasons for holding inventory.**

**Model Answer:**

**Inventory** is the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods, and supplies held by an organization to support production, sales, and operations.

**Four reasons for holding inventory:**

1. **To meet anticipated customer demand** — smooth out the gap between production and consumption.
2. **To decouple operations** — different stages can run independently with buffer stock.
3. **To protect against stockouts** — avoid lost sales and backorder penalties.
4. **To take advantage of quantity discounts** — order in bulk when unit price drops.

*(Accept any other valid reason: hedging against price inflation, pipeline transit, smoothing production, etc.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Continuous Review (Q, r) system and Periodic Review (R, S) system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Continuous Review (Q, r) | Periodic Review (R, S) |
|---|---|---|
| Review | Every transaction | Every R period |
| Order Quantity | Fixed (= Q) | Variable (= S − current stock) |
| Reorder Point | Fixed level r | Inventory topped to level S |
| Safety Stock | Lower (only lead-time variance) | Higher (variance over full R + L) |
| Information Need | Real-time tracking | Periodic count |
| Typical Use | High-value items | Grouped / retail items |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A manufacturing company consumes 18,000 units of a raw material per year. The cost of placing one order is ₹200 and the holding cost is ₹5 per unit per year. The lead time is 10 days and the company operates 300 days per year.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Determine the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), number of orders per year, and cycle time in days.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compute the reorder point, total annual inventory cost, and verify that ordering cost equals holding cost at the optimum.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 18,000 units/yr, K = ₹200, H = ₹5/unit·yr, L = 10 days, operating days = 300/yr.

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000} = 1200 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{18{,}000}{1200} = 15 \text{ orders per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle time:**

$$
T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1200}{18{,}000} = 0.0667 \text{ yr}
$$

Convert to days: $T = 0.0667 \times 300 = 20$ days per cycle.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 4 — Reorder point (ROP):**

Daily demand = D / operating days = 18,000 / 300 = 60 units/day.

$$
r = D_{\text{day}} \times L = 60 \times 10 = 600 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5 — Total annual cost:**

$$
TC = \sqrt{2 D K H} = \sqrt{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200 \times 5} = \sqrt{36{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Verify OC = HC:**

$$
OC = \frac{D K}{Q^{*}} = \frac{18{,}000 \times 200}{1200} = ₹3{,}000
$$

$$
HC = \frac{Q^{*} H}{2} = \frac{1200 \times 5}{2} = ₹3{,}000
$$

**Conclusion:** OC = HC = ₹3,000, confirming the cost-equality property at the EOQ.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A retail store sells 9,000 units of a product annually. The ordering cost is ₹100 per order, the holding cost is ₹3 per unit per year, and the lead time is 15 days. The store operates 360 days per year.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the EOQ, the optimal number of orders per year, and the time between orders.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Determine the reorder point, the minimum total cost, and compute the annual cost saving if the store currently orders 1500 units per order (instead of EOQ).**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 9,000 units/yr, K = ₹100, H = ₹3/unit·yr, L = 15 days, operating days = 360/yr.

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9{,}000 \times 100}{3}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} \approx 774.6 \text{ units} \approx 775 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \frac{9{,}000}{774.6} \approx 11.62 \text{ orders/yr} \approx 12 \text{ orders}
$$

**Step 3 — Time between orders:**

$$
T = \frac{774.6}{9{,}000} = 0.0861 \text{ yr} = 0.0861 \times 360 = 31 \text{ days}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 4 — Reorder point:**

Daily demand = 9,000 / 360 = 25 units/day.

$$
r = 25 \times 15 = 375 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum total cost:**

$$
TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 9{,}000 \times 100 \times 3} = \sqrt{5{,}400{,}000} = ₹2{,}323.79
$$

**Step 6 — Cost at Q = 1500 (current policy):**

$$
OC_{\text{current}} = \frac{9{,}000 \times 100}{1500} = ₹600
$$

$$
HC_{\text{current}} = \frac{1500 \times 3}{2} = ₹2{,}250
$$

$$
TC_{\text{current}} = 600 + 2{,}250 = ₹2{,}850
$$

**Step 7 — Annual saving by switching to EOQ:**

$$
\text{Saving} = TC_{\text{current}} - TC_{\min} = 2{,}850 - 2{,}323.79 = ₹526.21 \text{ per year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Forgetting to convert lead time:** If L is given in days, you MUST divide D by operating days to get daily demand. Many students incorrectly use r = D × L directly, giving r = 9,000 × 15 = 135,000 (which is absurdly large). **Always check units.**
>
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Not verifying OC = HC:** The KTU board examiner allocates **1 mark** explicitly for this verification. Skipping it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Using total cost formula incorrectly:** Some students write $TC = \frac{DK}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2} + C \cdot D$ which is fine when purchase cost is needed, but for the standard EOQ question, omit the C·D term as it is constant.
>
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Wrong rounding of Q\*:** Always round Q* to the nearest **integer** (number of units). If asked for the "optimal number of orders," divide unrounded D/Q\* and round only at the end. Do not round Q* and then re-divide — this introduces inconsistency.
>
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Forgetting to express cycle time in the right unit** (years vs. days). Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it above your study desk.**

- [ ] **Inventory** = stock of materials held to decouple supply from demand.
- [ ] **Inventory policy** decides **when** to order, **how much** to order, and **how to review**.
- [ ] **Three core costs:** Ordering cost (K), Holding cost (H), Shortage cost (rare in basic EOQ).
- [ ] **Average inventory** under instantaneous replenishment = **Q / 2**.
- [ ] **Number of orders per year** = D / Q; **Cycle time** = Q / D.
- [ ] **EOQ (Wilson's Formula):** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DK}{H}}$
- [ ] **Minimum total cost:** $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DKH}$
- [ ] **Cost-equality theorem:** At Q*, $OC = HC = \sqrt{DKH/2}$.
- [ ] **Reorder point (deterministic):** $r = D \times L$ (use *daily* demand with *daily* lead time).
- [ ] **Continuous Review (Q, r)** = fixed order, variable interval. Lower safety stock.
- [ ] **Periodic Review (R, S)** = fixed interval, variable order. Higher safety stock.
- [ ] **Holding cost rate i** typically 20–40% of unit cost per year; $H = i \times C$.
- [ ] **ABC Analysis:** A = tight control (10–20% items, 70–80% value); C = loose control.
- [ ] **Assumptions of basic EOQ:** known constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, fixed K and H, single item, infinite horizon.
- [ ] **Second-order condition** is automatically satisfied (convex TC), so Q* is always a global minimum.
- [ ] **Practice the chain:** Q\* → N → T → r → TC → verify OC = HC.
- [ ] **Watch the units:** D in units/year, L in days, T in years/days, TC in ₹/year.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lead time, Fixed order quantity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT504 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lead time, Fixed order quantity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lead Time (L)

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Lead time is the elapsed time between the moment an order is placed (or a replenishment request is initiated) and the moment the ordered quantity is received and becomes available in the inventory for use.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$L = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4$$

where:
- $L_1$ = Order preparation time (creating the purchase order)
- $L_2$ = Supplier processing / manufacturing time
- $L_3$ = Transit / shipping time
- $L_4$ = Receiving, inspection, and put-away time

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Concept:** Lead time is **deterministic** in basic models (a fixed known value $L$) and **stochastic** (random variable) in advanced probabilistic models. The 2024 Scheme Module 3 typically deals with **deterministic lead time** first.

### 1.2 Components of Lead Time — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pizza Delivery Analogy** 🍕
> Imagine you order a pizza at 7:00 PM.
> - **$L_1$** (Order Prep): The restaurant writing down your order → 5 min
> - **$L_2$** (Supplier Process): The chef making your pizza → 15 min
> - **$L_3$** (Transit): The delivery boy travelling to your home → 10 min
> - **$L_4$** (Receiving): You opening the box and checking it → 2 min
> - **Total Lead Time** = 32 minutes
>
> This is **Lead Time** in inventory — the total delay between *deciding to replenish* and *actually having stock in hand*.

### 1.3 Fixed Order Quantity System (FOQ / Q-System)

**Definition:**
The Fixed Order Quantity (FOQ) system, also called the **Q-System** or **Continuous Review System**, is an inventory control policy in which a **predetermined fixed quantity $Q$** is ordered **each time** the inventory position falls to or below a predefined **Reorder Point (ROP)**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Order Quantity | $Q$ | Fixed amount ordered each cycle |
| Reorder Point | $R$ | Inventory level that triggers a new order |
| Lead Time | $L$ | Time to receive replenishment |
| Demand Rate | $D$ | Units consumed per unit time |
| Safety Stock | $SS$ | Buffer against demand/lead-time variability |

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy for FOQ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Grocery Store Analogy** 🏪
> Think of a grocery store that always orders **exactly 50 cartons of milk** every time their stock drops to **20 cartons**.
> - The **50** = Fixed Order Quantity ($Q$)
> - The **20** = Reorder Point ($R$)
> - The **time between two orders** = Cycle Time ($T = Q/D$)
> - The store is **continuously watching** its stock (continuous review) and acts **only when the trigger is hit**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Flow of the Fixed Order Quantity System

The FOQ system operates on a precise **trigger-based cycle**:

1. **Monitor** the inventory position continuously (every transaction).
2. **Compare** current inventory position $IP$ with the reorder point $R$.
3. **IF** $IP \leq R$, place an order of fixed size $Q$.
4. **WAIT** for lead time $L$ to elapse.
5. **RECEIVE** exactly $Q$ units, which adds back to inventory.
6. **REPEAT** the cycle indefinitely.

### 2.2 Inventory Position vs. Inventory Level

This is a **critical KTU distinction** that examiners love to test.

$$\text{Inventory Position } (IP) = \text{On-Hand Inventory} + \text{On-Order Inventory} - \text{Backorders}$$

$$\text{On-Hand Inventory (OH)} = \text{Physical stock currently in the warehouse}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Reorder Point $R$** is checked against **Inventory Position**, NOT on-hand stock. This is because an order already in transit counts as available future supply.

### 2.3 Reorder Point Calculation

The reorder point must cover the demand expected **during the lead time**, plus a safety buffer:

$$R = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$$

where:
- $\bar{d}$ = Average demand rate (units/day)
- $L$ = Lead time (days)
- $SS$ = Safety stock

**Lead Time Demand (LTD):**

$$LTD = \bar{d} \cdot L$$

**Without safety stock** (deterministic case):

$$R = \bar{d} \cdot L$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + L_4$ | Total lead time | time |
| 2 | $LTD = \bar{d} \cdot L$ | Lead time demand | units |
| 3 | $R = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | Reorder point | units |
| 4 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DC_o}{C_h}}$ | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | units |
| 5 | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D}$ | Optimal cycle time | time |
| 6 | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | Optimal number of orders/year | orders/yr |
| 7 | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DC_oC_h}$ | Minimum total variable cost | ₹/year |
| 8 | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LTD}$ | Safety stock (service level approach) | units |
| 9 | $\sigma_{LTD} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^2}$ | Std. dev. of LTD (for constant $L$) | units |
| 10 | $IP_t = OH_t + OO_t - BO_t$ | Inventory position at time $t$ | units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Quick-Reference:**
> - $D$ = Annual demand
> - $C_o$ (or $A$) = Ordering cost per order
> - $C_h$ (or $iC$) = Holding cost per unit per year
> - $z$ = Standard normal value for desired service level
> - $\sigma_d$ = Std. deviation of daily demand

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FOQ system is heavily used in:
- **Manufacturing spare parts** inventory (aircraft, automobiles)
- **Pharmaceutical supply chains** — drug replenishment
- **E-commerce warehouses** (Amazon-style restocking)
- **Hospital blood banks** — fixed batch procurement
- **Industrial raw materials** — steel, chemicals, electronic components

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)

The Economic Order Quantity is the optimal fixed order quantity $Q^*$ that minimizes the **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost.

**Step 1: Define the cost components**

$$\text{Ordering Cost per year} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o$$

**Reasoning:** If annual demand is $D$ units and each order brings $Q$ units, then the number of orders placed per year is $D/Q$. Multiply by cost per order $C_o$.

$$\text{Holding Cost per year} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$$

**Reasoning:** Average inventory in a saw-tooth cycle is $Q/2$. Holding cost per unit per year is $C_h$. So annual holding cost is $Q/2$ times $C_h$.

**Step 2: Total Variable Cost function**

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot C_h}{2}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and set to zero**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $Q$**

$$\frac{C_h}{2} = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 D \cdot C_o}{C_h}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}}$$

**Step 5: Verify minimum using second derivative**

$$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (minimum confirmed)}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $Q^*$ back to get minimum total cost**

$$TC^* = \frac{D C_o}{\sqrt{2DC_o/C_h}} + \frac{C_h}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DC_o}{C_h}}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\boxed{TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem Statement:**
> A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units/year** of a raw material. The cost of placing one order is **₹150**, and the annual holding cost per unit is **₹6**. Lead time is **10 days**, and the firm operates **300 days/year**. Determine:
> (a) Economic Order Quantity $Q^*$
> (b) Optimal number of orders per year $N^*$
> (c) Optimal cycle time $T^*$
> (d) Minimum total variable cost
> (e) Reorder point $R$ (assume no safety stock)

**Given Data:**

$$D = 12{,}000 \text{ units/year}, \quad C_o = ₹150/\text{order}, \quad C_h = ₹6/\text{unit/year}$$

$$L = 10 \text{ days}, \quad \text{Working days} = 300 \text{ days/year}$$

---

**Solution (a): Economic Order Quantity**

Using the EOQ formula:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150}{6}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{600{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* \approx 774.6 \approx 775 \text{ units}}$$

---

**Solution (b): Optimal number of orders per year**

$$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6} \approx 15.49$$

$$\boxed{N^* \approx 15 \text{ orders/year}}$$

---

**Solution (c): Optimal cycle time**

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{774.6}{12{,}000} \text{ years} = 0.06455 \text{ years}$$

Converting to days:

$$T^* = 0.06455 \times 300 = 19.36 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{T^* \approx 19.36 \text{ days between orders}}$$

---

**Solution (d): Minimum total variable cost**

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150 \times 6}$$

$$TC^* = \sqrt{21{,}600{,}000} \approx ₹4{,}647.58$$

$$\boxed{TC^* \approx ₹4{,}648/\text{year}}$$

**Verification:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6} \times 150 = 15.49 \times 150 = ₹2{,}323.79$$

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{774.6}{2} \times 6 = 387.3 \times 6 = ₹2{,}323.79$$

$$\text{Sum} = ₹4{,}647.58 \checkmark$$

(Note: At optimum, ordering cost = holding cost — a classic KTU check.)

---

**Solution (e): Reorder Point**

First, compute daily demand rate:

$$\bar{d} = \frac{D}{\text{Working days}} = \frac{12{,}000}{300} = 40 \text{ units/day}$$

Then:

$$R = \bar{d} \cdot L = 40 \times 10 = 400 \text{ units}$$

$$\boxed{R = 400 \text{ units}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $Q^*$ | 775 units | Order 775 units each time |
| $N^*$ | 15 orders/yr | 15 replenishment cycles annually |
| $T^*$ | 19.36 days | Reorder every ~19 days |
| $TC^*$ | ₹4,648/yr | Minimum annual inventory cost |
| $R$ | 400 units | Trigger next order at 400 units |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time (Saw-Tooth Pattern)

The classic **saw-tooth wave** of inventory under FOQ is shown below. Notice that:
- Inventory depletes at rate $\bar{d}$ during the cycle.
- A new order arrives **exactly when stock reaches R** (in the deterministic case).

```
Inventory
Level
   |
 Q |-->--------------<-- Order placed at ROP
   |   \            ^
   |    \          /|  Q units arrive
   |     \        / |
   |      \      /  |
 R |-------*-----*---|----------- Time
   |        \   /    |
   |         \ /     |
  0|-----------*------|--------
           ROP    ROP+order
        triggered   received
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the FOQ System (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Cycle] --> B[Inventory at Maximum Q]
    B --> C{Check Inventory Position IP}
    C --> D[Demand occurs, IP decreases]
    D --> E{Is IP <= Reorder Point R?}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Place Order of Fixed Qty Q]
    F --> G[Wait for Lead Time L]
    G --> H[Order Received: IP increases by Q]
    H --> I[Cycle Resets to Max Q]
    I --> C
```

### 4.3 Lead Time Components Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Order Preparation L1] --> L2[Supplier Processing L2]
    L2 --> L3[Transit or Shipping L3]
    L3 --> L4[Receiving and Inspection L4]
    L4 --> L5[Total Lead Time L]
```

### 4.4 Cost Trade-off Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OC[Ordering Cost = D*Co/Q] --> SUM[Total Cost = OC + HC]
    HC[Holding Cost = Q*Ch/2] --> SUM
    SUM --> OPT[Optimal at Q* = sqrt 2D*Co/Ch]
    OC -.decreases with Q.-> OPT
    HC -.increases with Q.-> OPT
```

### 4.5 Decision Flow — Reorder Point Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Inventory Monitoring]) --> Calc[Compute IP = OH + OO - BO]
    Calc --> Compare{IP less than R?}
    Compare -->|Yes| Order[Place order of Q units]
    Compare -->|No| Wait[Continue consumption]
    Wait --> Calc
    Order --> WaitL[Wait L time units]
    WaitL --> Receive[Receive Q units]
    Receive --> Calc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Lead Time" in inventory management. List its four main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
